proposals.title: Diachronous Deep-Water Extinctions: Testing the Role of Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation Collapse Across a South Atlantic Depth Transect
proposals.motivation: The prevailing paradigm for the end-Pleistocene megafaunal extinction, influenced by Broecker's 'Great Ocean Conveyor' concept, often assumes synchronous global climatic forcing. However, recent lacustrine records from the Bonneville Basin reveal complex, regional hydrologic changes during the Mystery Interval, suggesting climate perturbations were not uniform. This demands a re-examination of marine extinction patterns, particularly in the climatically sensitive South Atlantic, a key region for deep-water formation and ocean mixing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the timing of marine biotic declines during the last deglaciation was diachronous with depth in the South Atlantic, directly linked to the vertical structure and pacing of Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) collapse and recovery, which differentially affected deep-water mass properties and ventilation.
proposals.method: We will analyze a depth transect (500m to 3500m) of South Atlantic sediment cores spanning the last 25,000 years. At each site, we will employ high-resolution radiocarbon dating on monospecific planktic foraminifera to establish a robust chronology. We will quantify extinction/decline timing using benthic foraminiferal assemblage counts and the abundance of key indicator species. To link biotic changes to water mass history, we will measure paired stable carbon isotopes (δ13C) on benthic foraminifera (a tracer of water mass aging and ventilation) and neodymium isotopes (εNd) on authigenic Fe-Mn oxyhydroxides (a tracer of deep-water provenance).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, depth-stratified record of marine biotic response to deglacial AMOC changes, moving beyond basin-averaged views. It will directly test the synchronicity assumption in paleoceanographic extinction events and offer a mechanistic framework linking ocean circulation physics to biological turnover. The findings will refine models of how future oceanographic changes might propagate through deep-sea ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While past work has examined Atlantic-wide changes, the novel integration of a precise depth transect with dual geochemical proxies (δ13C and εNd) allows us to disentangle the vertical progression of water mass alteration and its specific biotic impacts, a approach not previously applied to the South Atlantic extinction question.

proposals.title: Constraining Mid-Pleistocene Atlantic-Pacific Deep Water Mixing and Climate Dynamics via Sediment Proxy and Extinction Record Data Assimilation
proposals.motivation: The mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT) represents a fundamental shift in Earth's climate system, characterized by the emergence of high-amplitude, ~100-kyr glacial cycles. The role of deep ocean circulation, particularly the interplay between North Atlantic deep water formation and Pacific ventilation, in driving this transition remains poorly quantified. Proxy records from marine sediments and extinction events offer critical but underutilized constraints on past ocean states and their climatic impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that data assimilation of benthic foraminiferal δ¹³C (tracing deep water masses) and extinction records of deep-sea species (sensitive to water mass properties) into an intermediate complexity climate model will reveal that a systematic change in Atlantic-Pacific deep water mixing strength was a key driver of the increased climate sensitivity and longer glacial cycles observed during the MPT.
proposals.method: We will employ an Ensemble Kalman Filter (EnKF) data assimilation framework to merge proxy observations with the UVic Earth System Climate Model. The state vector will include ocean temperature, salinity, and circulation parameters. Observational constraints will be assimilated from a newly compiled global database of mid-Pleistocene sediment core proxies (δ¹³C, Cd/Ca) and curated extinction/presence records of benthic foraminifera. The EnKF will iteratively adjust the model's initial ocean state and mixing parameters to find an optimal fit to the paleo-observations across multiple glacial-interglacial cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dynamically consistent, data-constrained reconstruction of deep ocean circulation and water mass mixing during the MPT. It will quantitatively test the 'great ocean conveyor's' role in the climate transition and provide a novel, transferable framework for integrating disparate paleo-archives into climate models. The results will directly inform understanding of how ocean circulation modulates Earth's climate sensitivity.
proposals.novelty: While data assimilation is common in modern climatology, its application to deep-time paleoclimate, especially using extinction records as quantitative constraints on ocean physics, is pioneering. This moves beyond simple model-data comparison to a rigorous statistical inversion that directly estimates past ocean states and their uncertainties.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Benthic Foraminiferal Extinction in the Mid-Pleistocene Transition: A Global Carbon Cycle Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The Mid-Pleistocene Transition (MPT) represents a fundamental shift in Earth's climate rhythm, yet its ultimate cause remains unresolved. A recent hypothesis, grounded in paleoceanographic data, suggests the extinction of a key deep-water benthic foraminiferal functional group in the North Pacific altered the ocean's biological pump. This proposal seeks to rigorously test this hypothesis by scaling its potential impact from a regional extinction event to a global carbon cycle perturbation, directly addressing a core mystery in paleoclimatology.
proposals.hypothesis: The extinction of a deep-water, carbonate-dissolving benthic foraminiferal functional group in the North Pacific during the MPT significantly reduced pelagic carbonate burial efficiency, enhancing deep-ocean alkalinity and CO2 storage, thereby contributing to the observed shift towards longer, more intense glacial cycles.
proposals.method: We will employ a coupled ocean circulation-biogeochemical box model, building on the 'great ocean conveyor' framework, to simulate global carbon and alkalinity cycles. The model will be forced with MPT boundary conditions. The key experiment will involve removing the parameterized function of the target benthic group in the North Pacific box, altering the local calcite dissolution flux. We will use an inverse modeling approach to quantify the resulting changes in global carbonate compensation, deep ocean carbon storage, and atmospheric pCO2, comparing model outputs to proxy records across the MPT interval.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, mechanistic link between a specific biotic event and the global climate shift of the MPT. It will advance understanding of how deep-sea ecosystem changes can propagate through the Earth system, offering a novel biotic mechanism for Pleistocene climate evolution that complements existing physical hypotheses.
proposals.novelty: While the conveyor belt's physical role is well-studied, this proposal uniquely integrates a documented paleontological extinction into a global biogeochemical model to test its systemic climate impact, moving beyond correlation to a testable mechanism for the MPT.

proposals.title: Disentangling Climatic and Anthropogenic Drivers of Early Holocene Fire Regimes: A Multi-Proxy Study of the Atlantic-Pacific Conveyor Response
proposals.motivation: The early Holocene (11.7-8.2 ka) witnessed a significant global increase in biomass burning, a key Earth system transition. A major unresolved question is whether this was primarily driven by post-glacial climatic changes creating favorable fire conditions or by the expansion of human populations and their use of fire. Resolving this is critical for understanding natural climate-vegetation-fire feedbacks and the early anthropogenic footprint.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the early Holocene fire increase was fundamentally climate-driven, mediated by changes in atmospheric circulation and moisture transport linked to the re-organization of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and the Great Ocean Conveyor, rather than by human ignition alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel mixing of marine and terrestrial archives. High-resolution charcoal records from lacustrine sediments in the Bonneville Basin and other Great Basin sites will provide local fire history. We will correlate these with existing and new Atlantic and Pacific marine sediment core proxies (e.g., δ¹⁸O, ice-rafted debris) for AMOC strength and North Pacific water mass formation. A key technical approach will be to apply statistical cross-wavelet and Granger causality analyses to the time-series data to test for lead-lag relationships between ocean circulation changes, regional hydroclimate (from cave carbonates), and fire activity before and during periods of known human presence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, ocean-atmosphere framework for understanding hemispheric fire regime shifts, directly linking deep ocean circulation dynamics to continental climate and fire suitability. It will offer a quantitative test of the 'climate-first' vs. 'human-first' models for early Holocene environmental change, with implications for projecting future fire risks under AMOC changes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing the 'Great Ocean Conveyor' as a primary driver of continental fire patterns by statistically coupling high-resolution terrestrial fire archives with marine circulation proxies, moving beyond simple correlation to assess causation during a key climate transition.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Southern Ocean's Past: A Multi-Proxy Inverse Model to Test the Constancy of Ventilation Over the Last Millennium
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean's overturning circulation is a critical component of the global climate system, regulating heat, carbon, and nutrient distributions. Interpretations of key paleo-ventilation tracers like benthic-planktic radiocarbon differences and the P04* nutrient tracer for the last 800 years often rely on the simplifying assumption of constant ventilation strength, potentially obscuring its true climatic sensitivity. This assumption limits our understanding of how this key region responded to past climate variability, such as during the Little Ice Age or Medieval Climate Anomaly, with implications for projecting future change.
proposals.hypothesis: Southern Ocean ventilation over the past 800 years was not constant but varied in response to climatic forcings, specifically shifts in wind stress (e.g., Southern Annular Mode), buoyancy fluxes, and sea-ice dynamics. These variations are encoded and can be extracted from existing tracer records when constrained by independent climate proxies.
proposals.method: We will adapt the inverse deconvolution framework used to solve for variable gas-age-ice-age differences (Δage) in ice cores. A suite of independent, high-resolution paleoclimate proxy records sensitive to ventilation drivers—including sea-ice proxies (e.g., diatom assemblages, sea salt sodium), dust fluxes (from ice cores), and Southern Hemisphere temperature reconstructions—will serve as simultaneous forcing inputs. These will constrain a simplified physical-biogeochemical box model of Southern Ocean ventilation. Using a Bayesian Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) inversion algorithm, we will solve for the time-evolving ventilation rate that best fits the observed radiocarbon and P04* tracer records from sediment cores, thereby deconvolving the climatic signal from the tracer history.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, dynamically constrained reconstruction of Southern Ocean ventilation variability over the past millennium, moving beyond the constant-ventilation paradigm. It will directly test the sensitivity of deep water formation to specific climatic drivers, offering a new benchmark for climate models. The developed multi-proxy deconvolution framework will be a transferable tool for re-examining ventilation assumptions in other ocean basins and time periods.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly translating a successful ice-core chronostratigraphy solution (variable Δage deconvolution) to the analogous problem in ocean paleoclimatology. It moves beyond single-tracer interpretations by using a suite of external climate proxies as physical constraints in an inverse model, transforming passive geochemical tracers into dynamic variables within a coupled system.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating Southern Ocean Ventilation Dynamics Over the Last 800 Years: A Multi-Proxy Assessment of Variability
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is a critical component of the global ocean conveyor, acting as a primary region for the ventilation of deep waters and the sequestration of carbon and heat. The canonical view, based on tracers like radiocarbon and preformed phosphate (PO4*), often assumes relatively stable ventilation rates on centennial timescales. However, emerging paleoclimate records from terrestrial systems, like those from the Bonneville Basin, demonstrate significant hydroclimate variability during recent millennia, suggesting the ocean's response may also be more dynamic than previously modeled.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Southern Ocean ventilation has varied significantly over the past 800 years, and that apparent constancy in single-proxy marine records is an artifact of compensating changes in different water mass formation and mixing processes.
proposals.method: We will compile and analyze high-resolution, well-dated marine sediment core records from key Southern Ocean sectors (Atlantic and Pacific). The approach will combine established tracers: radiocarbon (Δ14C) from benthic foraminifera to directly assess ventilation age, and nutrient proxies (Cd/Ca, δ13C) to reconstruct PO4* and water mass signatures. A novel mixing model, adapted from Broecker's 'PO4*-C*' and 'radiocarbon-age' frameworks, will be applied to deconvolve the contributions of varying source water formation rates (e.g., Antarctic Bottom Water vs. Subantarctic Mode Water) and interior ocean mixing from the Atlantic and Pacific basins to the observed tracer fields.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, multi-proxy reconstruction of Southern Ocean ventilation variability, linking it to potential climate forcings like Southern Annular Mode shifts. It will challenge the paradigm of a static deep ocean overturning during the late Holocene and offer a new paleo-constraint for climate models, improving predictions of the ocean's future carbon uptake and heat storage capacity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly testing the assumption of constant ventilation—a foundational premise in many paleoceanographic studies—by applying a multi-basin, multi-tracer mixing analysis specifically to the under-constrained 'Mystery Interval' and late Holocene period, rather than focusing solely on glacial-interglacial transitions.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Deglacial Atlantic-Southern Ocean Seesaw: A Tracer-Sensitivity Inversion Framework Applied to Paleo-Circulation Proxies
proposals.motivation: The hypothesized 'seesaw' in deep water production between the North Atlantic and the Southern Ocean is a central but poorly quantified concept in paleoclimate, invoked to explain abrupt climate shifts during deglaciations. Current assessments rely on qualitative comparisons of disparate paleo-proxy records (e.g., δ13C, 231Pa/230Th, εNd), which often disagree due to unresolved non-conservative behaviors and water mass mixing effects. A rigorous, quantitative framework to reconcile these proxies and test the seesaw hypothesis is critically needed to understand past ocean circulation's role in climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: That the apparent discordance between modern ventilation tracers (CFC-11, 14C, PO4*) can be inverted to redefine their model sensitivities, and that applying this revised, physically-consistent tracer framework to deglacial proxy data will reveal a dominant, anti-phased mode of deep water production between the Atlantic and Southern Ocean basins.
proposals.method: We will first perform an inversion using modern oceanographic data (WOA, GLODAP) and a coarse-resolution ocean model to reconcile observed CFC-11 ventilation ages with simulated 14C and PO4* distributions. This inverse problem will be solved using an adjoint-based optimization to recalibrate the effective sensitivities and boundary conditions for the paleo-proxies in the model. This updated tracer system will then be embedded into a transient deglacial simulation (e.g., using the UVic ESCM or LOVECLIM). We will systematically compare the model's quantitative seesaw signal against a compiled synthesis of paleo-proxy records from the Atlantic and Southern Ocean, using the inverse model's transfer functions to directly translate model state variables into proxy space.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, unified tracer framework that moves paleo-circulation analysis beyond pattern matching to quantitative, physics-based proxy interpretation. It will provide the first tracer-constrained estimate of the amplitude and timing of the deglacial Atlantic-Southern Ocean production seesaw, directly testing its dominance as a mode of overturning variability. The recalibrated proxy sensitivities will also serve as a critical benchmark for future paleoceanographic studies and model-data comparisons.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit inversion of modern tracer discordance to *define* the model's paleo-proxy system, rather than tuning models to fit paleo-data directly. This creates a physically-consistent, transferable framework to quantitatively test the seesaw hypothesis, a significant advance over qualitative correlations.

proposals.title: Disentangling 20th-Century Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) Trends: Modern Anthropogenic Forcing versus Recovery from a Little Ice Age Maximum
proposals.motivation: A leading hypothesis posits that the observed 20th-century slowdown of the AMOC represents a recovery from a stronger state during the Little Ice Age (LIA), driven by natural variability. This framing diminishes the perceived role of modern anthropogenic forcing (greenhouse gases, aerosols) on this critical climate system component. Resolving this ambiguity is essential for accurate attribution of ocean circulation changes and for projecting future AMOC stability under continued warming.
proposals.hypothesis: The 20th-century AMOC slowdown is primarily a forced response to modern anthropogenic climate change, not a recovery from a LIA maximum. We hypothesize that proxy records will show no coherent, basin-wide evidence for a significantly stronger AMOC during the LIA compared to the preceding Medieval Climate Anomaly.
proposals.method: We will apply Broecker's expertise in water mass mixing and paleoceanographic tracers to synthesize and critically re-evaluate existing proxy archives of AMOC strength (e.g., δ13C, Pa/Th, grain size) from the North Atlantic deep-water formation regions and the deep Pacific. A key technical approach will be to use an optimal fingerprinting algorithm to formally separate the simulated responses to natural (volcanic, solar) and anthropogenic forcings in last-millennium climate model ensembles (e.g., PMIP3/CMIP5), comparing these to the spatio-temporal pattern of changes in the multi-proxy network. This will test whether the observed 20th-century trend aligns with a forced anthropogenic signal or a model-simulated internal recovery from a LIA state.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, multi-proxy and model-based test of the LIA-recovery hypothesis for AMOC variability. It will clarify the primary drivers of recent ocean circulation change, directly informing climate attribution studies. The synthesis will also identify critical gaps in the proxy network for constraining past AMOC strength, guiding future paleoceanographic fieldwork.
proposals.novelty: While the LIA-recovery idea is discussed, a formal detection and attribution study targeting this specific hypothesis using a combination of last-millennium model simulations and a broad proxy synthesis has not been conducted. The novelty lies in framing the question as a signal separation problem within the context of the 'Great Ocean Conveyor' stability.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Buoyancy and Mixing Controls on Atlantic-Southern Ocean Ventilation Coupling: A Water Mass Transformation Analysis
proposals.motivation: The bipolar seesaw hypothesis, central to interpreting abrupt climate events like Dansgaard-Oeschger cycles, posits an anti-phase relationship between deep water formation in the North Atlantic and Southern Ocean. However, the specific physical mechanisms that enforce, modulate, or break this coupling remain poorly quantified. Understanding these controls is critical for assessing the stability of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and its role in past and future climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the coupling between Atlantic and Southern Ocean ventilation is not a fixed seesaw but a dynamic state governed by the balance of surface buoyancy forcing and interior diapycnal mixing in each basin, with Pacific stratification acting as a key modulator of inter-basin connectivity.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchy of models, from idealized sector models to the fully-coupled CESM, applying the Walin (1982) water mass transformation framework. This diagnostic will be used to quantitatively partition the volume transformation contributing to deep ventilation into components driven by surface buoyancy fluxes, interior diapycnal mixing, and isopycnal processes in the Atlantic and Southern Ocean sectors. We will systematically test sensitivities by performing perturbation experiments (e.g., altering Southern Hemisphere westerly wind stress, North Atlantic freshwater forcing, and Pacific pycnocline structure) and analyzing the resultant changes in transformation budgets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, physics-based quantification of the drivers of inter-hemispheric ventilation coupling, moving beyond conceptual models. It will deliver a testable framework for interpreting paleo-proxy records of deep water circulation and offer new metrics for evaluating the realism of coupled climate model simulations of overturning dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While the bipolar seesaw is a well-established concept, applying a rigorous water mass transformation analysis to explicitly diagnose the coupling mechanism in models is novel. This approach directly links the canonical 'mixing' and 'formation' paradigms in oceanography to the problem of inter-basin climate teleconnections.

proposals.title: Constraining the 20th-Century Weakening of the Southern Ocean's Lower Cell: A Multi-Proxy Data Assimilation Reconstruction
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is the primary nexus for the global overturning circulation, yet its centennial-scale variability remains poorly constrained. Direct observational records are too short to assess whether recent changes in deep water formation and Antarctic Bottom Water export are anomalous within the context of the last millennium, hindering our ability to attribute changes to anthropogenic forcing versus natural climate modes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Southern Ocean's lower overturning cell experienced a significant, spatially heterogeneous weakening during the 20th century that exceeds the range of natural variability reconstructed over the preceding 500-1000 years, driven by anthropogenic warming and freshening of high-latitude surface waters.
proposals.method: We will build a strategic network of high-resolution sediment cores from the Atlantic and Pacific Southern Ocean, analyzing 231Pa/230Th (bottom current speed), Nd isotopes (water mass provenance), and sortable silt. These will be synchronized with ice core sea-salt aerosol fluxes (wind/wave activity) and tree-ring constrained atmospheric CO2 growth rates. An ensemble Kalman filter data assimilation framework will fuse these heterogeneous proxies with a lightweight, geometry-preserving ocean box model to invert for overturning strength and water mass export, producing probabilistic, time-evolving reconstructions with quantified uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially explicit, probabilistic reconstruction of Southern Ocean overturning variability over the past millennium, directly quantifying 20th-century change. It will provide critical paleo-validation for climate models and isolate the fingerprint of anthropogenic forcing on this key component of the global ocean conveyor.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of deep-water sediment proxies with integrative atmospheric archives (CO2, aerosols) within a formal data assimilation framework moves beyond qualitative comparisons to produce a physically consistent, quantitative reconstruction that can distinguish basin-scale overturning changes from local hydrographic noise.

proposals.title: Crossdating Southern Ocean Sediment Cores: A Dendrochronological Test of 20th-Century Deep Water Formation Variability
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is a critical component of the global ocean conveyor, with deep water formation driving global heat and carbon exchange. Observational records are too short to determine if reported 20th-century changes represent a unique anthropogenic signal or natural multi-decadal variability. High-resolution paleo-records are needed, but are limited by dating uncertainties from methods like 210Pb and 14C, which obscure precise comparisons of event timing across cores.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying dendrochronological crossdating techniques to ultra-high-resolution sediment proxies will reveal that the 20th century did not exhibit a unique, sustained slowdown in Southern Ocean deep water formation, but rather an amplification of variability consistent with pre-industrial climate modes.
proposals.method: We will obtain a network of high-accumulation sediment cores from the Weddell Sea, Ross Sea, and Antarctic Peninsula margins. Using Itrax XRF core scanning at 100-micron resolution and diatom assemblage counts, we will extract annual-layer proxy signals (e.g., Si/Ti ratios for diatom blooms, Fe/Ca for lithogenic input). The COFECHA algorithm, adapted for sediment series, will perform pattern-matching of these high-frequency variations across cores to synchronize them and build a master chronology for the last 500 years, anchored by absolute dates from known volcanic tephra layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first absolutely dated, multi-proxy network of Southern Ocean surface productivity and particle flux for the last half-millennium, with annual-to-decadal resolution. It will provide a rigorous, error-minimized test of 20th-century deep water formation changes, directly informing models of ocean circulation sensitivity under anthropogenic forcing.
proposals.novelty: The novel transfer of the dendrochronological crossdating paradigm to marine sediments, treating annual sediment layers as analogous to tree rings, is a radical departure from standard radiometric dating approaches. This method explicitly solves the core problem of precise temporal alignment that has limited high-resolution paleoceanographic synthesis.

proposals.title: Testing the Trigger: Internal Ocean Dynamics vs. External Forcing in Driving Abyssal Ventilation Reorganizations During the Little Ice Age
proposals.motivation: The Little Ice Age (LIA, ~1300-1850 CE) represents the most recent significant natural cooling event, providing a critical test case for understanding ocean-climate coupling. While the LIA's surface climate is documented, the response of the deep ocean's overturning and ventilation—key components of the global conveyor—remains ambiguous. Disentangling whether deep ocean changes were a direct, forced response to cooling or emerged from intrinsic oceanic variability is essential for projecting the stability of modern overturning under anthropogenic forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rapid shifts in Southern Ocean abyssal ventilation during the LIA were not a simple linear function of external cooling but were instead triggered or amplified by internal ocean dynamical thresholds, such as changes in eddy kinetic energy or deep western boundary current instabilities.
proposals.method: We will generate new high-resolution (sub-decadal) proxy records of bottom-water ventilation (Δ14C, δ13C of benthic foraminifera) and hydrography (Mg/Ca, B/Ca) from precisely dated Southern Ocean sediment cores. These empirical reconstructions of ventilation rate and variability will be compared to two suites of Last Millennium ensemble simulations from the Community Earth System Model (CESM): one with full external forcing (volcanic, solar) and one with fixed pre-industrial forcing to isolate internal variability. Spectral and wavelet coherence analyses will be used to statistically link proxy-recorded abrupt shifts to model-simulated dynamical events in eddy fields and current pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic assessment of the drivers of deep ocean change during the LIA, directly quantifying the partition between forced and intrinsic variability. It will advance paleoceanography by moving beyond mean-state reconstructions to analyze the statistics of abrupt change, offering a new framework for interpreting past ocean variability in climate models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in reframing the LIA not just as a cold period but as a natural experiment in ocean dynamical thresholds, using a combined proxy-model approach specifically designed to isolate internal oceanic processes from external climate forcing, a distinction rarely attempted in paleo-studies of this era.

proposals.title: Beyond the Seesaw: A Bayesian Quantification of NADW-AABW Phase Relationships Across Pleistocene Climate Transitions
proposals.motivation: The 'bipolar seesaw' hypothesis posits an anti-phase relationship between North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) and Antarctic Bottom Water (AABW) production, a key dynamic of the global ocean conveyor. However, paleo-records and modern observations suggest a more complex spectrum of possible interactions, including synchronous changes and variable lags. Systematically characterizing this full range of phase relationships is critical for accurately modeling past climate transitions and projecting the ocean's response to future forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the phase relationship between NADW and AABW production is not statically anti-phase but varies predictably with the background climate state and the nature of the forcing, with a true anti-phase seesaw emerging only under specific boundary conditions during major reorganizations of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation.
proposals.method: We will integrate a perturbed-parameter ensemble of the Community Earth System Model (CESM) simulations, spanning key Pleistocene transitions, with a newly compiled network of high-resolution proxy records (Nd isotopes, δ13C, sortable silt) from Atlantic and Southern Ocean sediment cores. A Bayesian hierarchical model, implemented via Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, will be used to infer the dominant spatial-temporal modes of deep water production co-variability from the joint proxy-model dataset, quantifying probabilities for synchronous, anti-phase, lagged, or uncorrelated behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first probabilistic framework for deep water production relationships, moving from a binary seesaw test to a continuous quantification of interaction modes. It will deliver a calibrated, multi-proxy synthesis for both basins and identify the specific climatic thresholds that trigger a transition between different dynamical regimes of the global overturning circulation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit, statistical search for the full spectrum of phase relationships—not just the seesaw—by jointly inverting a climate model ensemble and a radiometrically constrained proxy network within a formal Bayesian framework, an approach not yet applied to this classic problem.

proposals.title: Tephrochronological Synchronization of Atlantic and Southern Ocean Ventilation Proxies Across the Last Glacial Termination
proposals.motivation: The 'bipolar seesaw' hypothesis, central to Broecker's ocean conveyor concept, posits that changes in North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation are antiphased with changes in Southern Ocean ventilation, driving abrupt climate shifts. However, pre-2026 tests of this hypothesis are hampered by large and often uncertain relative age models between marine sediment cores from the two basins, making it difficult to establish precise lead-lag relationships and the true phasing of the seesaw.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during the last deglaciation, reductions in NADW formation, as recorded in North Atlantic sediment proxies, were precisely synchronized via globally deposited volcanic sulfate layers to coeval increases in Southern Ocean ventilation, with a lag of less than two centuries, providing a direct empirical validation of the interhemispheric ventilation seesaw.
proposals.method: We will use precisely dated volcanic sulfate horizons from the WAIS Divide and Greenland ice cores as isochronous tie-points. These will be matched to cryptotephra layers or geochemical signatures in a transect of high-sedimentation-rate marine cores from the North Atlantic (e.g., IRD belt) and the Atlantic sector of the Southern Ocean. Once synchronized on this common tephrochronological framework, we will compare high-resolution time-series of ventilation proxies (benthic δ13C for water mass geometry, authigenic Nd isotopes for water mass sourcing, and benthic-planktic 14C age offsets for ventilation rate) from both basins across the Mystery Interval and Bølling-Allerød/Younger Dryas transitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first directly synchronized, quantitative record of Atlantic-Southern Ocean deep water exchange dynamics across a major climate transition. It will rigorously test the bipolar seesaw mechanism by providing unambiguous constraints on its phasing and magnitude, thereby refining our understanding of the ocean's role in abrupt climate change.
proposals.novelty: While volcanic markers have been used regionally, their systematic application as a global 'Rosetta Stone' to synchronize high-resolution ventilation proxies between the polar oceans on absolute timescales represents a novel, direct test of conveyor belt dynamics, moving beyond model-inferred phasing.

proposals.title: Testing the Mechanistic Link Between Northern Ice-Rafting and Southern Ocean Ventilation: A Multi-Proxy and Modeling Study of the Mystery Interval
proposals.motivation: The 'bipolar seesaw' hypothesis, central to understanding abrupt climate change, posits that freshwater forcing in the North Atlantic can slow deepwater formation and trigger Southern Ocean warming and ventilation changes. However, the precise phasing and mechanistic causality of this interhemispheric teleconnection remain ambiguous, often relying on correlations from poorly synchronized records. Establishing a direct, physical link is critical for validating climate models and understanding the global ocean's role in past and future climate variability.
proposals.hypothesis: Freshwater pulses from Northern Hemisphere ice-rafting events (e.g., Heinrich events) during the last deglaciation directly force a synchronous reduction in Southern Ocean ventilation via a rapid atmospheric and/or oceanic teleconnection, rather than being co-responses to a common external forcing.
proposals.method: We will generate precisely synchronized proxy records from strategically located Southern Ocean sediment cores. High-resolution ice-rafted debris (IRD) counts will identify freshwater event timings, while paired epibenthic foraminiferal δ13C (water mass chemistry) and carefully cleaned Δ14C (ventilation age) will quantify ventilation changes. These records will be synchronized using common tephra layers and high-resolution δ18O stratigraphy. The empirically constrained phasing and magnitude will then drive freshwater perturbation experiments in the UVic Earth System Climate Model (an intermediate-complexity model with a 3D ocean GCM and dynamic-thermodynamic sea ice), using passive tracers to track Southern Ocean water mass pathways and response times.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-proxy, mechanistically tested evaluation of the ice-rafting forcing hypothesis for Southern Ocean ventilation changes. It will deliver a robust empirical chronology for key deglacial events in the Southern Ocean and quantify the sensitivity of Southern Ocean overturning to Northern forcing in a climate model directly informed by paleo-data.
proposals.novelty: The study moves beyond correlation by using synchronous, process-specific proxies from the same cores to establish precise phasing, and then employs this empirical constraint to design a causal model test, isolating the teleconnection mechanism from other drivers.

proposals.title: Coupling of Southern Ocean Ventilation and North Atlantic Ice-Rafting: A Test of the Bipolar Seesaw During the 1500-Year Climate Cycle
proposals.motivation: The 1500-year ice-rafting cycle, a persistent feature of glacial climates, is thought to be linked to changes in Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) strength, as conceptualized in the 'great ocean conveyor.' However, the role of Southern Ocean ventilation, a critical control on deep water formation and global ocean mixing, in pacing or responding to these Northern Hemisphere events remains poorly constrained. Testing this link is essential for validating the bipolar seesaw hypothesis and understanding the global oceanic teleconnections that drive abrupt climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that slowdowns in Southern Ocean ventilation, indicated by reduced air-sea gas exchange and deep water formation, are systematically coupled to the ice-rafting cycle, specifically occurring during or immediately following phases of intensified Heinrich-like iceberg discharge in the North Atlantic.
proposals.method: We will generate high-resolution, radiocarbon-dated records of past Southern Ocean ventilation from carefully selected sediment cores in the Atlantic and Pacific sectors of the Southern Ocean, targeting the last glacial period. Ventilation ages will be reconstructed using the paired benthic-planktic radiocarbon method, applying a Bayesian age-modeling framework (e.g., Bacon or OxCal) to synchronize these records with established North Atlantic ice-rafted debris (IRD) stratigraphies. Spectral and cross-wavelet analysis will be used to test for phase-locking between the ventilation proxy time series and the 1500-year IRD cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, radiocarbon-based test of Southern Ocean ventilation changes across multiple 1500-year cycles, quantifying the phasing relationship with Northern Hemisphere forcing. It will critically evaluate the role of the Southern Ocean as an amplifier or pacemaker of millennial-scale climate variability, adding a crucial southern component to the conveyor belt paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While the conveyor belt and bipolar seesaw are established concepts, directly testing the phase relationship between Southern Ocean ventilation proxies and the precise rhythm of North Atlantic ice-rafting events at this timescale is novel. This moves beyond correlation to establish a testable mechanistic sequence within the global ocean-climate system.

proposals.title: Speleothem Crossdating: A Dendrochronological Framework for Synchronizing High-Resolution Paleoclimate Records from Stalagmites
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean's ventilation, a critical component of the global ocean conveyor, is modulated by wind-driven upwelling. Reconstructing past wind variability requires precisely dated, high-resolution climate proxies. Speleothems offer such records via δ¹⁸O and trace elements, but individual chronologies can be uncertain, and aligning multiple records to extract a coherent regional signal remains a methodological challenge, limiting our ability to resolve rapid climate events like those during the Mystery Interval.
proposals.hypothesis: The statistical pattern-matching principles of dendrochronological crossdating can be successfully adapted to synchronize speleothem geochemical time-series, enabling the construction of robust, absolutely dated master chronologies that correct for non-climatic growth disturbances and provide a more reliable reconstruction of past atmospheric circulation.
proposals.method: We will apply cross-correlation and pattern-matching algorithms, including the COFECHA software framework from dendrochronology, to high-resolution δ¹⁸O and Sr/Ca series from multiple precisely U-Th dated stalagmites from climatically sensitive cave sites in the Southern Hemisphere (e.g., South Pacific islands). The method will involve detrending individual series to isolate high-frequency climate signals, then statistically matching these patterns across records to identify synchronous events, validate individual chronologies, flag potential hiatuses, and generate a composite master series.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, transferable methodological standard for aligning speleothem records, increasing confidence in the timing and phasing of past climate events. It will yield a validated, high-fidelity reconstruction of Southern Hemisphere westerly wind variability, directly testing hypotheses about wind-driven ventilation changes in the Southern Ocean across key climate transitions.
proposals.novelty: While speleothem scientists use statistical alignment, the formal, rigorous framework of dendrochronological crossdating—with its systematic approach to pattern matching, error-checking, and master chronology building—has not been explicitly transferred and adapted to the speleothem paleoclimate community as a core methodology.

proposals.title: Fjord-Scale Process Analogues for the Atlantic-Southern Ocean Seesaw: A Coupled Modern and Paleo Study
proposals.motivation: The Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and Southern Ocean upwelling are hypothesized to operate in a seesaw, with anti-phased deep water formation driving major climate shifts. However, the precise atmospheric and hydrological forcings that trigger these switches remain poorly constrained in modern observations and paleo-records. Testing this fundamental mechanism directly in the open ocean is logistically challenging due to its vast scale and integration of multiple processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fjord and marginal sea systems, which exhibit rapid switches between estuarine (inflow at depth) and anti-estuarine (outflow at depth) circulation modes, are dynamic, reduced-scale analogues for the Atlantic downwelling and Southern Ocean upwelling modes, and that their sedimentary archives preserve a high-fidelity record of past switching behavior.
proposals.method: We will instrument modern fjords in Greenland and Norway (e.g., Scoresby Sund, Sognefjorden) exhibiting known circulation switches with moorings to measure high-resolution salinity, temperature, currents, and oxygen isotopes to quantify the freshwater and heat forcing triggering mode changes. Concurrently, we will collect precisely dated sediment cores from the same basins. We will apply established geochemical proxies (e.g., δ¹⁸O of foraminifera, ice-rafted debris counts, neodymium isotopes) and novel lacustrine-cave-calibrated methods for paleohydrology to reconstruct past circulation states. The modern process data will be used to validate a high-resolution regional ocean model (e.g., MITgcm), which will then be forced with paleo-boundary conditions to test for anti-phased behavior between instrumented basins over the last deglaciation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first directly observed, mechanistic link between specific climatic forcings and switches in deep water formation regimes. It will produce a tightly coupled, process-calibrated paleo-record of circulation changes, offering a new validation dataset for the parameterization of ocean mixing and deep water formation in global climate models (e.g., CESM, GFDL) simulating the Atlantic-Southern Ocean seesaw.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating fjords not just as archives, but as real-world process laboratories where the complete forcing-response-sediment signal chain of the oceanic seesaw can be observed and quantified at an accessible scale, bridging the gap between modern oceanography and paleoclimatology in a single, targeted study.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Homeostatic Role of North Atlantic Deep Water Formation in Response to Southern Ocean Forcing During the Mystery Interval
proposals.motivation: The 'great ocean conveyor' is a key component of Earth's climate system, but its dynamics are often described as passive responses to forcing. The Mystery Interval (17.5-14.5 kyr BP) provides a critical test case, where Southern Ocean changes may have perturbed global circulation. Understanding whether the Atlantic response was a passive consequence or an active, stabilizing feedback is crucial for predicting the system's resilience to future perturbations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that changes in North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation during the Mystery Interval acted as a compensatory, homeostatic feedback to Southern Ocean slowdowns, actively modulating the global impact on heat redistribution and carbon storage, rather than merely changing concurrently.
proposals.method: We will first synthesize high-resolution paleoclimate proxy records (e.g., Pa/Th, δ13C, neodymium isotopes) from Atlantic and Southern Ocean cores to establish the phasing and rate of circulation changes. We will then conduct targeted experiments using the Community Earth System Model (CESM) with a water-mass-tracking module. A Southern Ocean perturbation will be induced, and model analysis will focus on quantifying the sensitivity, rate, and lag of the Atlantic response, using metrics analogous to feedback gain and time constants in control theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond correlative descriptions of ocean circulation change to a mechanistic, quantitative framework for ocean-climate feedbacks. It will provide specific parameters for the Atlantic's compensatory capacity, offering a new paradigm for interpreting paleo-records and assessing the stability of the modern overturning circulation under anthropogenic forcing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a homeostatic systems physiology analogy to ocean dynamics, explicitly testing for compensatory feedback rather than concurrent change, and quantifying feedback parameters that are not part of standard paleoceanographic analysis.

proposals.title: A Phylogenetic Comparative Framework for Testing Interglacial AMOC Ventilation States
proposals.motivation: Projections of future Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) stability rely heavily on paleo-analogs, particularly past warm interglacials like MIS 5e. However, the uniqueness of each interglacial's orbital and ice-sheet forcing complicates direct analog selection. A robust, statistical framework is needed to test whether AMOC slowdown signatures are contingent on specific, repeatable forcing thresholds across different interglacial 'lineages', moving beyond simple pairwise comparisons.
proposals.hypothesis: That interglacial AMOC ventilation states, inferred from deep-water proxy records, are not randomly distributed but are statistically associated with specific, quantifiable nodes on a 'climate state tree' representing shared forcing ancestries and bifurcations.
proposals.method: We will construct a directed, dated 'climate state tree' for interglacials (MIS 1, 5e, 7, 9, 11, 19) based on their sequence and divergence in key forcing parameters (e.g., insolation patterns, residual ice volume). Ventilation strength data (δ13C of benthic foraminifera, 231Pa/230Th from North Atlantic cores) will be mapped as continuous traits onto the tree tips. We will then apply phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS) and phylogenetic independent contrasts (PIC) models to test for correlations between ventilation and forcing variables while accounting for shared evolutionary history, adapting algorithms like those in the R package 'caper'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a statistically rigorous method for identifying which past interglacials are true functional analogs for future AMOC behavior under specific forcing pathways. It will directly test the 'Great Ocean Conveyor' sensitivity to interglacial boundary conditions, offering a new, phylogenetically-informed scaling law for paleo-constraints on circulation thresholds.
proposals.novelty: The explicit adaptation of phylogenetic comparative methods, a cornerstone of evolutionary biology, to solve the problem of trait (ventilation state) correlation across non-independent paleoclimate 'taxa' (interglacials) represents a novel cross-disciplinary synthesis. This moves beyond descriptive analogies to a hypothesis-testing framework for paleoceanography.

proposals.title: Ice-Provenance Fingerprinting of Interglacial Conveyor Slowdowns: Testing a Recurring Sequence of Laurentide and Fennoscandian Ice Sheet Instability
proposals.motivation: The stability of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) is a critical uncertainty for future climate, with paleoclimate records suggesting it weakened significantly during past warm periods like the Last Interglacial (MIS 5e). However, the specific triggers of these slowdowns remain ambiguous, hindering our ability to assess their recurrence. This work addresses whether a consistent, geographically specific mechanism—ice sheet discharge from vulnerable sectors—paced interglacial ocean ventilation changes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that slowdowns in North Atlantic deep-water formation during MIS 5e and MIS 11 were consistently triggered by freshwater discharge from specific, recurring ice sheet sectors (e.g., the Hudson Strait or Fennoscandian ice streams), as recorded by the provenance of ice-rafted debris (IRD).
proposals.method: We will apply high-resolution sediment provenance techniques to IRD layers in North Atlantic sediment cores spanning MIS 5e and MIS 11. The method centers on geochemical fingerprinting (e.g., Sr-Nd-Pb isotopes, detrital carbonate petrography) of the coarse lithic fraction to pinpoint the geological source of icebergs. This provenance data will be stratigraphically correlated with established proxies for ventilation strength (e.g., δ13C of benthic foraminifera, Pa/Th ratios) to test for a direct, causal sequence between discharge from specific ice sheet catchments and subsequent reductions in deep-water formation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first process-based, provenance-constrained test of ice sheet-ocean coupling during past warm intervals. It will determine if a recurring sequence of instability is a fundamental pacing mechanism for interglacial climate, moving beyond correlation to establish causation. The findings will directly inform which modern ice sheet sectors pose the greatest risk for future AMOC disruption.
proposals.novelty: While IRD provenance is well-established for glacial periods, its systematic application to diagnose the triggers of interglacial circulation anomalies is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk freshwater forcing to identify the precise geographical origin of meltwater pulses, testing a specific mechanistic hypothesis about recurring climate dynamics.

proposals.title: Reassessing the Glacial Tropical Ocean: A Test of Stable Seawater δ¹⁸O-Salinity Relationships During the Last Glacial Maximum
proposals.motivation: The δ¹⁸O of foraminifera is a primary proxy for past ice volume and ocean temperature, but its interpretation relies on assumed stable regional relationships between seawater δ¹⁸O (δ¹⁸O_sw) and salinity. This assumption underpins paleoceanographic reconstructions of the 'great ocean conveyor' and its role in climate change. If these relationships were significantly different in the tropical oceans during glacial periods, due to altered atmospheric circulation and hydrological cycles, it would challenge fundamental constraints on glacial ocean salinity gradients and deep water formation.
proposals.hypothesis: The linear δ¹⁸O_sw-salinity relationship in the tropical Atlantic and Pacific Oceans was non-stationary during the Last Glacial Maximum, deviating from the modern spatial regression due to a reorganization of precipitation-evaporation patterns and ocean mixing.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-proxy, multi-core approach targeting the tropical Atlantic and Pacific. Paired measurements of Mg/Ca (for temperature) and δ¹⁸O on surface-dwelling foraminifera (Globigerinoides ruber) will be used to deconvolve δ¹⁸O_sw. Salinity will be independently constrained using the alkenone unsaturation index (U₃₇ᴷ') as a salinity proxy where applicable, and by analyzing the oxygen isotope composition of fluid inclusions in lacustrine cave carbonates from the Bonneville Basin and other Great Basin archives. These terrestrial records provide a direct, quantitative paleohydrologic (δ¹⁸O of precipitation) constraint on the atmospheric moisture source feeding the tropical oceans, allowing us to test the marine-derived δ¹⁸O_sw-salinity slopes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct test of glacial δ¹⁸O_sw-salinity stability in the tropics, a critical but untested foundation of paleoceanography. It will quantify potential biases in glacial sea surface salinity and ice volume estimates. The findings will directly inform models of glacial ocean circulation and the mechanisms driving the 'Mystery Interval' and other abrupt climate events.
proposals.novelty: While past studies assume constant relationships, this proposal explicitly challenges this by integrating marine proxy deconvolution with quantitative terrestrial paleohydrology from cave carbonates—a novel archive Broecker has recently highlighted—to constrain the atmospheric driver of ocean surface isotopic change.

proposals.title: Microhabitat Bias in Benthic Foraminifera: Assessing the Fidelity of Deep North Atlantic Temperature Proxies
proposals.motivation: Benthic foraminiferal Mg/Ca and δ¹⁸O are foundational archives for reconstructing deep-ocean temperature and circulation, critical for understanding the 'great ocean conveyor' and its role in past climate change. However, if these organisms lived in localized microenvironments (e.g., influenced by porewater chemistry, microbial activity, or sediment mixing) with temperatures or isotopic compositions atypical of the overlying bottom water, our core paleoceanographic records could be systematically biased. This directly questions the representativeness of key datasets used to model deep-water formation and ocean heat content during events like the Mystery Interval.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that benthic foraminifera species used for deep-temperature reconstructions in the North Atlantic inhabited microhabitats whose geochemical conditions, particularly temperature, were significantly offset from the ambient bottom water, leading to a non-representative paleoceanographic signal.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-proxy, site-specific approach focusing on core-top and late Holocene sediments from key North Atlantic deep-water formation regions. First, we will use high-resolution micro-sampling (laser ablation ICP-MS) to analyze intra-test Mg/Ca and δ¹⁸O variability within individual foraminifera shells, testing for heterogeneities indicative of vital effects or microenvironmental influence. Second, we will compare paired Mg/Ca and clumped isotope (Δ₄₇) measurements on the same samples, as Δ₄₇ is a more direct temperature proxy less susceptible to vital effects. Third, we will analyze the stable carbon isotope (δ¹³C) gradient between infaunal and epifaunal species and porewater modeling to quantify microhabitat offsets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing 'microhabitat bias' in benthic foraminiferal proxies, potentially revising error estimates for deep-ocean temperature reconstructions. It will yield a critical, process-based evaluation of the fidelity of records used to benchmark ocean circulation models, strengthening our understanding of past ocean heat uptake and water mass mixing during climatic transitions.
proposals.novelty: While vital effects are acknowledged, systematically testing for a consistent temperature offset in deep-water foraminifera due to their microhabitat—and quantifying it using the emerging clumped isotope paleothermometer paired with traditional methods—is a novel and direct test of a fundamental assumption in paleoceanography.

proposals.title: Constraining Glacial Tropical Pacific SSTs and Ocean Conveyor Dynamics Using Coral Sr/Ca Thermometry
proposals.motivation: The role of tropical sea surface temperatures (SSTs) in global climate during glacial periods remains poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of the 'Great Ocean Conveyor' and its links to abrupt climate events like the Mystery Interval. Existing marine sediment proxies (e.g., Mg/Ca, alkenones) have significant uncertainties in glacial environments. An independent, high-resolution SST record from the tropics is critical for testing hypotheses about ocean-atmosphere coupling and heat transport during these pivotal periods.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fossil corals from the Last Glacial Maximum and the Mystery Interval will yield Sr/Ca-derived SSTs significantly cooler than modern, and that these tropical temperature changes were tightly coupled to the strength and geometry of Atlantic meridional overturning circulation (AMOC).
proposals.method: We will apply coral Sr/Ca thermometry to well-preserved fossil *Porites* sp. specimens from the tropical Pacific (e.g., Tahiti, Vanuatu) dated to target glacial periods via U-Th geochronology. Sr/Ca ratios will be measured using high-precision inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) with careful standardization and replicate analysis. The temperature calibration will use a published, site-specific modern coral Sr/Ca-SST relationship, corrected for potential glacial seawater Sr/Ca changes using coupled coral-benthic foraminiferal records. Resulting SST time series will be statistically compared with Atlantic sediment core records of AMOC strength and North Atlantic Deep Water formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, seasonally-resolved SST estimates from the glacial tropical Pacific using corals, offering a critical benchmark for climate models. It will directly test the coupling between tropical heat content and high-latitude climate events, refining our understanding of the ocean conveyor's operation during glacial states and its role in global climate instability.
proposals.novelty: While Sr/Ca thermometry is established for Holocene reconstructions, its rigorous application to glacial-age corals to interrogate ocean conveyor dynamics is novel. This approach moves beyond local temperature reconstruction to explicitly link Pacific warmth to Atlantic deep-water formation and extinction events, a core Broecker theme.

proposals.title: Partitioning the Oceanic Anthropogenic CO2 Sink: A Transient Tracer and Biogeochemical Budget for the Atlantic and Pacific Conveyor End-Members
proposals.motivation: The ocean's 'solubility pump' and 'biological pump' are the dominant mechanisms sequestering anthropogenic CO2, but their relative strengths and regional contributions remain poorly quantified. This partitioning is critical for predicting future atmospheric CO2 levels and ocean acidification, as these pumps respond differently to climate change. A direct, observation-based quantification, particularly across the key Atlantic and Pacific basins which represent contrasting limbs of the global ocean conveyor, is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the physical solubility pump currently dominates the anthropogenic CO2 inventory in the deep North Atlantic, while the biological pump contributes a significantly larger fraction in the deep North Pacific, reflecting the contrasting ventilation ages and nutrient regimes of these conveyor end-members.
proposals.method: We will construct detailed carbon budgets along hydrographic sections in the deep North Atlantic (formation region) and North Pacific (extinction region) using pre-2026 transient tracer data (CFCs, SF6) and carbon system measurements (DIC, Δ14C, δ13C). The method involves using CFC ages to separate the anthropogenic DIC increase from natural variability. A mixing analysis will deconvolve water mass contributions, allowing us to attribute the anthropogenic CO2 inventory in each basin to physical (solubility-driven) uptake versus biological (soft-tissue and carbonate) export and remineralization, using preformed phosphate and alkalinity relationships.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first observationally constrained, basin-scale quantification of the physical versus biological anthropogenic CO2 sink. It will deliver crucial validation targets for next-generation Earth System Models and inform projections of how the ocean carbon sink may evolve as stratification and circulation change. The results will directly test conceptual models of the conveyor's role in carbon storage.
proposals.novelty: While the conveyor belt concept is established, directly partitioning the anthropogenic carbon inventory within its key limbs using transient tracers as a dating tool is novel. This approach moves beyond theoretical estimates and global models to provide a data-driven, process-oriented budget for the two most critical oceanic regions for carbon sequestration.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Glacial-Interglacial Variability of the Atlantic Biological Pump via Inverse Modeling of Carbon Isotope and Concentration Data
proposals.motivation: The strength of the ocean's biological carbon pump is a critical but poorly constrained variable in the global carbon cycle, especially during past climate transitions. Wallace Broecker's 'great ocean conveyor' concept highlights the central role of North Atlantic deep water formation in global circulation, but the coupling of this physical pump to the biological pump's efficiency remains quantitatively unclear. Reconstructing past pump strength is essential for understanding the partitioning of carbon between the atmosphere and the deep ocean during glacial-interglacial cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strength of the biological pump in the North Atlantic, a key region for deep water formation and carbon sequestration, varied systematically between glacial and interglacial periods, and that this variability can be quantitatively deduced from the isotopic (δ13C) and concentration gradients of dissolved inorganic carbon in the deep Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse model based on a simplified 3-box ocean-atmosphere system (Atlantic surface/deep, Pacific deep) to solve for the biological pump transfer rate. The model will be constrained by paleo-data: atmospheric CO2 from ice cores, and deep-ocean δ13C and Cd/Ca from benthic foraminifera. Using a Monte Carlo Markov Chain (MCMC) algorithm, we will invert the steady-state equations of the carbon cycle to find the pump parameter values that best fit the observed glacial and interglacial data states, explicitly accounting for mixing and air-sea exchange processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, data-constrained estimates of biological pump strength changes between the Last Glacial Maximum and the Holocene. It will directly test hypotheses about the role of nutrient utilization and carbon sequestration in driving atmospheric CO2 changes. The results will refine our mechanistic understanding of how the physical 'conveyor' and the biological pump interact to regulate climate.
proposals.novelty: While forward models have explored pump scenarios, this is a formal inverse approach to extract a key metabolic rate of the Earth system from paleo-proxy data. It uniquely combines Broecker's core themes of deep ocean mixing and Atlantic-Pacific contrasts to solve for a fundamental biogeochemical variable.

proposals.title: The Pacific Conveyor: Quantifying North Pacific Deep Water Formation and its Role in Deglacial Carbon Release
proposals.motivation: The canonical 'Great Ocean Conveyor' paradigm posits that the North Atlantic and Southern Ocean are the primary drivers of deglacial climate and carbon cycle changes through deep water formation and ventilation. However, emerging evidence suggests the North Pacific may have played a more active role during the last deglaciation, particularly during the 'Mystery Interval' (17.5-14.5 kyr BP). A systematic test of the Pacific's capacity to store and release carbon is needed to evaluate the completeness of the Atlantic-centric model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enhanced North Pacific Intermediate Water (NPIW) formation and subsequent deep water ventilation during the early deglaciation acted as a significant, previously underappreciated pathway for the release of respired carbon, necessitating a re-evaluation of traditional deglacial carbon cycle drivers.
proposals.method: We will apply geochemical mixing models (e.g., end-member analysis using εNd and δ13C) to high-resolution sediment cores from the Northwest Pacific to quantify past water mass composition and ventilation age changes. This will be coupled with novel measurements of boron isotopes (δ11B) in planktic foraminifera from the same cores to reconstruct subsurface pH and pCO2, directly tracing carbon outgassing. The core sites will be strategically located along the modern NPIW formation region and its southward flow path to map the spatial and temporal evolution of this process during the deglaciation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, multi-proxy geochemical evidence for the North Pacific's role as an active source of deglacial CO2. It will quantify the Pacific's contribution to the atmospheric carbon budget during key transitions like the Mystery Interval, offering a more holistic view of the global ocean's role in climate change. The results will challenge or refine the existing conveyor belt schematic.
proposals.novelty: While the North Atlantic's role is well-established, directly testing the Pacific's capacity for deep water formation and carbon release during the deglaciation using a combined ventilation-pH proxy approach is novel. It moves beyond correlation to mechanistic proof, applying methods proven in the Atlantic to the under-sampled Pacific system.

proposals.title: Constraining North Atlantic Convection Shutdown and Recovery During the Mystery Interval Using High-Resolution Boron Isotopes in Deep-Water Foraminifera
proposals.motivation: The 'Mystery Interval' (17.5-14.5 kyr BP) was a period of major global climate reorganization, potentially linked to a slowdown or shutdown of North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation as part of the great ocean conveyor. Existing paleoceanographic proxies for ocean carbon chemistry during this abrupt event are limited, creating uncertainty in the magnitude of pH and carbonate ion changes in the deep Atlantic, which are critical for understanding the ocean's role in atmospheric CO2 rise.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a detailed boron isotope (δ¹¹B) record from carefully selected deep-dwelling planktic foraminifera will reveal a significant, transient shoaling of the calcite saturation horizon and a decrease in deep-water pH in the North Atlantic during the Mystery Interval, directly constraining the carbon chemistry consequences of reduced convection.
proposals.method: We will generate a high-resolution (centennial-scale) δ¹¹B record across the Mystery Interval from monospecific samples of the deep-dwelling planktic foraminifer *Globorotalia truncatulinoides* (dextral) in a North Atlantic sediment core bathed by NADW. Samples will be cleaned using a rigorous oxidative/reductive protocol and analyzed via multi-collector inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (MC-ICP-MS) with careful standard bracketing. The δ¹¹B data will be converted to seawater pH using established pre-2026 calibrations and paired with benthic δ¹³C and B/Ca measurements to deconvolve the effects of water mass mixing versus local carbon chemistry change.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, direct record of deep-water pH evolution in the North Atlantic across this critical transition, offering a crucial test for ocean carbon cycle models simulating convection shutdown. It will directly link changes in ocean circulation (formation) to the carbon chemistry of the deep ocean, refining our understanding of the ocean's biogeochemical response to abrupt climate change.
proposals.novelty: While boron isotopes have been applied to surface pH reconstructions, applying this proxy to deep-water foraminifera to trace the chemical footprint of a deep convection event is novel. This approach moves beyond qualitative circulation tracers to provide a quantitative constraint on the carbon system perturbation, a key missing piece in Mystery Interval paleoceanography.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of North Pacific Deep Water Formation in Global Carbon Sequestration and Isotopic Evolution During the Last Glacial Maximum
proposals.motivation: The 'great ocean conveyor' paradigm, central to paleoclimatology, posits that North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation is the primary driver of deep ocean circulation and carbon storage. However, emerging paleoceanographic evidence, including data from the North Pacific and terrestrial archives like the Bonneville Basin, suggests significant deep water formation may have occurred in the North Pacific during glacial periods, a region currently characterized as a deep-water terminus. The impact of this hypothesized North Pacific Deep Water (NPDW) formation on the global carbon cycle, particularly its capacity to sequester CO2 and alter ocean isotopic inventories, remains a critical, unquantified gap in our understanding of glacial-interglacial climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that enhanced NPDW formation during the Last Glacial Maximum acted as a major carbon sink, significantly increasing the ocean's total carbon storage and imparting a distinct isotopic (δ13C) signature to the deep Pacific that can reconcile existing proxy data discrepancies.
proposals.method: We will employ the Community Earth System Model (CESM) version 1.3, a state-of-the-art coupled climate model with a fully interactive carbon cycle (iCESM). A series of LGM simulations will be conducted, systematically varying the surface buoyancy forcing in the North Pacific to induce different rates of deep convection and NPDW formation. We will use passive tracers and isotope-enabled modules to track the resulting water masses, carbon transport, and δ13C evolution. The analysis will focus on quantifying changes in global ocean carbon inventory, air-sea CO2 flux, and the three-dimensional distribution of δ13C, directly comparing model outputs to existing paleo-proxy records from the Atlantic and Pacific basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, model-based quantification of NPDW's role in the glacial carbon cycle, directly testing the 'conveyor belt' paradigm's completeness. It will deliver concrete estimates of NPDW's contribution to glacial CO2 drawdown and produce a new, physically consistent framework for interpreting deep ocean δ13C records, potentially resolving long-standing puzzles regarding glacial Pacific nutrient and carbon chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have focused on Atlantic processes, this proposal directly targets the Pacific's active role in deep water formation—a concept supported by paleo-data but never rigorously tested in a comprehensive Earth system model with interactive isotopes. It moves beyond descriptive box models to a dynamic, spatially explicit assessment of NPDW's global biogeochemical impact.

proposals.title: Constraining Atlantic-Pacific Deep Water Mixing and Provenance During the Mystery Interval Using Paired Foraminiferal Nd and O Isotopes
proposals.motivation: The 'Mystery Interval' (17.5-14.5 ka) was a period of abrupt climate reorganization, potentially linked to changes in the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and deep ocean water mass structure. Understanding the provenance and mixing of deep waters between the North Atlantic and Pacific basins during this interval is critical for testing hypotheses about the operation of the global ocean conveyor and its role in abrupt climate change, as proposed by Broecker's work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Mystery Interval featured a significant increase in the proportion of southern-sourced or Pacific-derived deep waters in the North Atlantic basin, as a direct consequence of reduced North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation, which will be recorded by radiogenic neodymium (Nd) isotope signatures in authigenic phases of benthic foraminifera.
proposals.method: We will apply a paired-analysis approach on the same samples of carefully cleaned benthic foraminifera (e.g., Cibicidoides wuellerstorfi) from North Atlantic sediment cores spanning the Mystery Interval. For each sample, we will first perform traditional oxygen isotope (δ¹⁸O) analysis to establish the chronostratigraphic framework and general hydrographic context. Subsequently, we will chemically leach the authigenic oxide coatings from the same foraminiferal specimens to extract the seawater-derived Nd signal, followed by precise measurement of the ¹⁴³Nd/¹⁴⁴Nd ratio (expressed as εNd) using thermal ionization mass spectrometry (TIMS). This direct pairing on single foraminifera isolates the deep-water mass provenance signal (εNd) within a precise climatic phase (δ¹⁸O).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, high-resolution record of deep-water provenance changes in the North Atlantic across the Mystery Interval, quantitatively testing the 'conveyor belt' reduction hypothesis. It will establish a novel geochemical benchmark for water mass mixing during a canonical abrupt climate event, directly linking ocean circulation changes to the hemispheric hydrologic shifts documented in Broecker's recent lacustrine and Great Basin paleoclimate studies.
proposals.novelty: While Nd isotopes have been used in bulk sediments, applying paired Nd-O isotope analyses on the same foraminiferal specimens to trace water mass provenance during a specific, abrupt climate event is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond inferring circulation from carbon isotopes or δ¹⁸O alone, providing a more direct water mass 'fingerprint' for a key interval in Broecker's conveyor paradigm.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Global Carbon Cycle Response to North Pacific Deep Water Formation Events: A Modeling Study of Ocean Conveyor Sensitivity
proposals.motivation: The canonical 'great ocean conveyor' concept emphasizes Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) as the primary driver of global ocean ventilation and carbon redistribution. However, paleo-records from the North Pacific, such as those from the Bonneville Basin, suggest significant regional hydrographic changes during past climate transitions like the Mystery Interval. The potential for North Pacific deep water formation to act as a global-scale carbon pump, complementing or perturbing the Atlantic system, remains a critical but poorly constrained component of the paleoclimate carbon cycle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transient initiation of deep water formation in the North Pacific would significantly alter the global ocean's carbon inventory and ventilation age patterns by reorganizing deep ocean circulation pathways and sequestering carbon away from the atmosphere on centennial to millennial timescales.
proposals.method: We will employ the data-constrained ocean inverse model of Gebbie & Huybers (pre-2026) to establish a modern baseline circulation and carbon fields. Using the MITgcm ocean circulation model, we will implement a suite of perturbation experiments where deep convection in the subarctic North Pacific is induced under Last Glacial Maximum boundary conditions. We will track the evolution of radiocarbon-derived ventilation ages and dissolved inorganic carbon (DIC) inventories globally using Lagrangian particle tracking algorithms embedded within the model's velocity fields, quantifying changes in carbon sequestration and deep ocean residence times.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, process-based assessment of the global carbon cycle impact of North Pacific deep water formation. It will deliver a new metric for the sensitivity of the ocean's carbon storage to overturning changes outside the Atlantic, offering a mechanistic framework to interpret Pacific paleo-proxy records of ventilation and hydrography within a global context.
proposals.novelty: While past studies focus on the Atlantic's role, this proposal explicitly tests the global-scale efficacy of a Pacific-centric deep water formation event as a driver of carbon redistribution, directly linking regional paleo-archives to the dynamics of the full ocean conveyor.

proposals.title: Deciphering the Deglacial Conveyor Belt: A Paired Radiocarbon and Paired Isotope (δ¹³C-Δ¹⁴C) Study of Southern Ocean and North Pacific Ventilation Phasing
proposals.motivation: The 'great ocean conveyor' is a primary modulator of global climate, yet the sequence of its reactivation during deglaciation remains ambiguous. Pre-2026 evidence suggests both the Southern Ocean and the North Pacific were key sources of deep water during the Last Glacial Maximum, but their relative roles and timing during the deglacial transition are poorly constrained, limiting our understanding of carbon cycle and heat transport feedbacks.
