proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that deglacial ventilation increased first and most strongly in the Southern Ocean, acting as the primary trigger for renewed Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), while North Pacific ventilation changes were a secondary, lagged response to this Southern-sourced reorganization.
proposals.method: We will apply paired measurements of benthic foraminiferal δ¹³C and Δ¹⁴C in precisely dated sediment cores from key locations: the Atlantic sector of the Southern Ocean (to monitor Southern-sourced water) and the deep Northwest Pacific. High-resolution radiocarbon analyses will be conducted via Accelerator Mass Spectrometry (AMS). The Δ¹⁴C of the deep water will be reconstructed by correcting the measured ¹⁴C age of benthic foraminifera for surface reservoir ages derived from paired planktonic ¹⁴C ages. The temporal evolution of these ventilation proxies will be aligned via synchronized age models using volcanic tephra layers and δ¹⁸O stratigraphy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first directly comparable, radiocarbon-based ventilation histories for the two major non-Atlantic deep-water formation regions across the last deglaciation. It will quantify the phasing and magnitude of their changes, offering a mechanistic test for hypotheses of deglacial AMOC recovery and identifying the dominant driver of the deep ocean's deglacial carbon release.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have examined each basin in isolation, this proposal's novelty lies in the systematic, parallel application of the Δ¹⁴C ventilation proxy in both oceans using identical methods, enabling a rigorous test of their relative timing—a critical unknown in conveyor belt dynamics.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Local Biogeochemical Forcing of Deep Convective Mixing in the Subarctic North Pacific
proposals.motivation: The North Pacific is a critical component of the global ocean conveyor, yet its deep mixing processes and their local biogeochemical impacts are less understood than those in the Atlantic. Recent observations suggest deep convection events can inject nutrients and oxygen into the surface layer, potentially triggering phytoplankton blooms and altering carbon export. Understanding these localized effects is essential for predicting how changes in ocean stratification and ventilation may impact regional productivity and carbon cycling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discrete, deep convective mixing events in the Subarctic North Pacific drive significant, localized pulses of primary production and carbon export by rapidly supplying limiting nutrients to the euphotic zone. This effect is modulated by the pre-existing ecological community and the intensity/depth of the mixing.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-resolution (eddy-permitting) coupled physical-biogeochemical ocean model (e.g., ROMS-NEMURO) to downscale a known deep convection region in the North Pacific. The model will be forced with high-frequency atmospheric reanalysis data (ERA5) to realistically simulate mixing events. Lagrangian particle tracking will be used to trace water parcels and nutrient pathways during convection. We will analyze model outputs against historical hydrographic and satellite chlorophyll data (SeaWiFS, MODIS) to validate the simulated biogeochemical response.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based understanding of how physical mixing directly controls local ecology and carbon flux in a key high-latitude region. It will quantify the 'convective pump' as a carbon export mechanism for the North Pacific, contributing to more accurate regional carbon budgets. The findings will inform interpretations of paleo-records of productivity changes linked to past ocean ventilation shifts.
proposals.novelty: While deep convection is studied for its physical role in the conveyor, its immediate, local biogeochemical consequences in the nutrient-rich but iron-limited North Pacific are not well quantified. This study uniquely bridges Broecker's conveyor concept with modern high-resolution modeling to examine ecological effects at the scale of the mixing event itself.

proposals.title: Tracing the Conveyor's Pulse: Lagrangian Attribution of Deep North Atlantic Freshening to Greenlandic and Arctic Source Anomalies
proposals.motivation: Observations indicate a significant freshening of deep North Atlantic water masses, a potential precursor to a slowdown of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) as conceptualized in Broecker's 'Great Ocean Conveyor.' However, the causal links between specific high-latitude freshwater sources (e.g., Greenland melt, Arctic sea ice export) and the heterogeneous freshening patterns in the deep ocean remain correlative. A mechanistic understanding of how these anomalies are advected and mixed along their pathways is critical for predicting the stability of the AMOC and its global climatic impacts.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed non-uniform freshening of North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) is structured by distinct, source-specific Lagrangian pathways, with Greenland meltwater anomalies primarily transported via boundary currents and dense overflows, while Arctic export anomalies are advected through the interior subpolar gyre.
proposals.method: We will use the high-resolution (1/10°) MITgcm (ECCOv4 configuration) to simulate ocean circulation and passive freshwater tracers for key source regions (Greenland, Arctic, precipitation) over the 1992-2025 period. A Lagrangian particle-tracking system (e.g., Parcels) will be initialized in these source regions and advected offline using the 5-day mean 3D velocity fields. By analyzing the evolving density and geographical pathways of particle ensembles, we will quantify the fractional contribution of each source to salinity anomalies in defined NADW density classes, directly testing the advective structuring hypothesis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic, pathway-based attribution of deep-ocean freshening, moving the field from statistical correlation to dynamical causation. It will quantify the sensitivity of key deep-water formation regions to individual freshwater forcings, offering critical constraints for climate models and projections of AMOC stability under future warming.
proposals.novelty: While freshwater hosing experiments are common, this study uniquely employs a high-resolution, source-tagged Lagrangian framework to disentangle the real-world, concurrent contributions of multiple freshwater sources along their actual advective routes, a method not yet applied to the contemporary deep freshening problem.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Forcing Role of Ocean Freshening: A Detection and Attribution Study from the 8.2ka Event to the Modern Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation
proposals.motivation: The role of freshwater fluxes in abrupt climate change, exemplified by the 8.2ka event and Heinrich events, is well-established in paleoclimatology. However, in contemporary climate change, ocean freshening from ice sheet melt and hydrological cycle intensification is predominantly treated as a feedback. This creates a critical gap in understanding whether modern freshening can act as a primary forcing mechanism, independently perturbing the climate system by altering deep-water formation and the global ocean conveyor.
proposals.hypothesis: Large-scale ocean freshening can act as a primary climate forcing agent, and its distinct climatic signature—characterized by a specific pattern of Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) weakening and interhemispheric heat redistribution—can be statistically detected and attributed separately from greenhouse gas forcing in both paleo-records and 21st-century projections.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchy of models, from idealized box models to the fully-coupled Community Earth System Model (CESM1). A formal detection and attribution (D&A) framework, using optimal fingerprinting with multi-model ensembles (e.g., CMIP5), will be applied. First, we will simulate the 8.2ka event using freshwater hosing experiments to derive a 'freshening forcing' fingerprint. This fingerprint will then be used to statistically separate the impact of modern observed and projected freshening from other forcings in historical and future climate simulations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic, quantitative assessment of ocean freshening as an independent climate forcing. It will directly test the applicability of paleo-mechanisms to the modern climate, providing a rigorous, statistical basis for evaluating the risk of freshwater-driven AMOC changes in climate projections and policy-relevant scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While freshwater hosing is a standard paleo-experiment, formally applying climate D&A methodologies—developed for greenhouse gases—to isolate the signal of anthropogenic freshening is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond correlation to establish causation for freshening as a primary forcing agent in the modern system.

proposals.title: Identifying the Hypothalamic Triggers of the Climate System: Thresholds and Locations of Freshwater Forcing for Abrupt Circulation Change
proposals.motivation: Paleoclimate records, including those from the Mystery Interval and Heinrich events, demonstrate that rapid freshwater inputs to the North Atlantic are linked to abrupt climate shifts. However, the prevailing paradigm treats ocean freshening primarily as a feedback within a temperature-driven system. This project challenges that view by investigating whether specific freshwater fluxes act as primary, initiating drivers capable of setting the climate system onto a new trajectory, analogous to releasing hormones in neuroendocrinology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exist geographically specific freshwater flux thresholds, particularly in the subpolar North Atlantic and Nordic Seas, which act as non-linear triggers, decisively altering deepwater formation and the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC), thereby initiating global climate reorganization.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchy of ocean models, from idealized box models to the high-resolution GFDL Modular Ocean Model (MOM), to conduct sensitivity experiments. Freshwater will be injected at key locations (e.g., Labrador Sea, Greenland-Scotland Ridge) in controlled hosing experiments. We will systematically vary the flux magnitude, duration, and location to map the non-linear response surface of the AMOC, using established metrics like overturning strength at 26.5°N. Paleo-data from the Bonneville Basin and other archives will provide boundary conditions and validation for simulated climate states.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a quantitative framework for evaluating freshwater forcing as a primary climate driver, moving beyond its treatment as a passive feedback. It will deliver specific, testable thresholds for policymakers regarding ice melt and runoff. The neuroendocrine analogy provides a powerful conceptual shift for understanding abrupt change, directly building on Broecker's 'Great Ocean Conveyor' paradigm by identifying its control points.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the standard cause-effect assumption to treat targeted freshening as an active trigger, not a slow response, and in applying a precise medical-systems analogy to the climate problem. This focuses the investigation on the specific 'where' and 'how much' needed to force a regime shift.

proposals.title: Disentangling Trend from Oscillation: Quantifying the Global Carbon Cycle Impact of Multi-Decadal Southern Ocean Ventilation Variability
proposals.motivation: The Southern Ocean is a critical cog in the global ocean conveyor, dominating the oceanic uptake of anthropogenic CO2. Observational and modeling studies up to 2015 suggest its ventilation and overturning may have slowed in recent decades, but it is unclear if this is a long-term trend or part of a multi-decadal oscillation linked to modes like the Southern Annular Mode (SAM). This ambiguity introduces major uncertainty into projections of future atmospheric CO2 levels and climate risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-decadal oscillatory slowdown in Southern Ocean ventilation will produce atmospheric CO2 anomalies with a fundamentally different magnitude, spatial fingerprint in ocean carbon storage, and persistence than a monotonic, trend-like slowdown of equivalent amplitude, leading to a significant over- or under-estimation of carbon budget uncertainty if the oscillation is misinterpreted.
proposals.method: We will use the Community Earth System Model (CESM) with its full biogeochemical component (BEC). A control simulation with 20th-century forcing will establish baseline variability. Two primary perturbation experiments will be conducted: 1) a sustained, monotonic weakening of Southern Ocean deep water formation and mixing, and 2) a sinusoidal, multi-decadal oscillation of the same integrated magnitude in these processes, phased with observed SAM/IPO indices. We will analyze the resulting anomalies in atmospheric CO2, oceanic DIC inventories, and air-sea CO2 fluxes, comparing their evolution and spatial patterns using empirical orthogonal function (EOF) analysis and lag-correlation techniques.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly quantify the portion of observed and projected carbon cycle variability attributable to oscillatory versus trend-driven changes in Southern Ocean dynamics. It will provide a quantitative framework for assessing carbon budget risk and inform the interpretation of paleo-records, like those from Bonneville Basin, where distinguishing climate trends from oscillations is equally crucial.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have examined the carbon cycle response to a slowdown, this work uniquely isolates and contrasts the specific signatures of oscillatory versus trend-driven ventilation changes, a critical distinction for both future projections and paleoclimate interpretation that has not been systematically quantified.

proposals.title: Quantifying Seasonal Sampling Bias in the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation and Deep Water Formation Trends
proposals.motivation: Long-term ocean climate trends, critical for understanding the stability of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) and deep water formation, are often derived from ship-based hydrographic sections repeated at multi-year intervals, typically in summer. This seasonal sampling introduces an unknown bias and uncertainty, as it aliases strong intra-annual variability in properties like temperature, salinity, and dissolved oxygen. A rigorous, empirical quantification of this bias is essential to correctly interpret observed changes in the ocean's deep water masses and their role in the global climate system.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic subsampling of continuous, year-round oceanographic records to mimic summer-only occupation will reveal statistically significant biases in calculated long-term trends for key physical and biogeochemical properties in deep water formation regions, leading to over- or under-estimation of climate-driven changes.
proposals.method: We will utilize high-resolution, multi-year time series from existing instrumented mooring arrays (e.g., OSNAP in the subpolar North Atlantic, RAPID at 26°N) and profiling float (Argo) data in the North Pacific and Southern Ocean. A complete climatological and trend baseline will be established from these full records. We will then apply a Monte Carlo subsampling algorithm that mimics the temporal pattern of historical ship surveys (e.g., summer-only, or specific months). For each of 10,000 iterations, trends will be calculated from the subsampled data and compared to the 'true' trend from the full record to compute bias and expanded uncertainty intervals.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirically derived correction factors and uncertainty penalties for trend analyses based on seasonally biased hydrographic data. It will deliver a diagnostic framework to identify regions and variables (e.g., deep oxygen in the North Pacific) most vulnerable to seasonal aliasing, directly informing the design of future observing systems and the re-interpretation of past climate records from ocean sediments.
proposals.novelty: While the problem of seasonal sampling is conceptually acknowledged, this study moves beyond critique to provide a quantitative, generalizable error analysis specific to deep ocean climate trends, linking modern observational gaps to paleoceanographic interpretations of water mass history and extinction events.

proposals.title: Reconciling Tracer-Derived Ventilation Timescales in the Deep Atlantic: The Role of Intrinsic Ocean Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Modern tracers like CFC-11 suggest decadal-scale ventilation of the deep North Atlantic, while integrated tracers like radiocarbon and P04* indicate centennial-scale mean ages. This discordance complicates our understanding of the ocean's role in climate, particularly the stability and variability of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). Resolving this timescale mismatch is critical for interpreting paleo-proxy records and predicting the ocean's response to anthropogenic forcing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the observed multi-tracer timescale discordance is driven by internal ocean dynamics, specifically rapid, localized events—such as shifts in deep western boundary currents or abyssal water mass injections—that imprint strongly on short-lived transient tracers while leaving a smoothed signature on integrated tracers.
proposals.method: We will use a high-resolution (eddy-permitting) ocean general circulation model (e.g., MITgcm or NEMO) configured for the Atlantic basin to simulate internal variability over a 200-year period. The model will be forced with repeated seasonal cycles to isolate intrinsic dynamics. We will implement online passive tracers with time histories mimicking CFC-11 and radiocarbon, and compute P04* from simulated phosphate and oxygen fields. Lagrangian particle tracking will be used to diagnose pathways and timescales of deep water formation and transport, focusing on the Labrador and Nordic Seas overflows and their boundary current systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, dynamics-based framework for interpreting disparate ocean ventilation tracers, moving beyond a purely advective-diffusive paradigm. It will directly test the hypothesis that rapid deep ocean property changes can occur without external climatic forcing, offering a new interpretation for events like those seen in the paleo-record of the 'Mystery Interval'.
proposals.novelty: While tracer discrepancies are known, existing studies often attribute them to model biases or averaging. This proposal uniquely posits that the discordance itself is a real signal of intrinsic ocean variability, linking modern observational oceanography with paleoceanographic concepts of abrupt deep ocean change.

proposals.title: Deconvolving the Source of North Atlantic Freshening: A Multi-Tracer Fingerprinting Approach
proposals.motivation: Observations indicate a significant freshening of the subpolar North Atlantic, a region critical for deep water formation and the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). This freshening could be driven by multiple sources—Arctic runoff, Greenland Ice Sheet melt, or enhanced precipitation—each with distinct climatic implications for ocean circulation and global heat transport. Identifying the dominant source is essential for predicting the stability of the AMOC and understanding the ocean's role in abrupt climate change, a central theme in Dr. Broecker's work on the 'great ocean conveyor'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Arctic runoff, Greenland ice melt, and precipitation-derived freshwater each carry a unique, diagnostic multi-isotope and geochemical signature, allowing for the quantitative apportionment of their contributions to the observed North Atlantic salinity anomaly.
proposals.method: We will adapt forensic sediment provenance techniques to the marine realm. First, we will compile a source signature library by analyzing δ¹⁸O, δD, dissolved silica, nutrient ratios (e.g., NO₃/PO₄), and lithogenic isotopes (e.g., εNd, ⁸⁷Sr/⁸⁶Sr) from known end-members: Greenland meltwater streams, major Arctic rivers (Mackenzie, Lena), and local precipitation. Second, we will apply an optimal multi-parameter mixing model (e.g., a Monte Carlo simulation within a Bayesian framework) to deconvolve these signatures in seawater samples from the deep water formation regions of the Labrador and Irminger Seas, collected on existing hydrographic cruises.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative partition of freshwater sources driving North Atlantic freshening, directly informing models of AMOC sensitivity. It will establish a novel, transferable framework for fingerprinting water masses, with applications to paleoceanography (e.g., interpreting Mystery Interval events) and future climate projections.
proposals.novelty: While individual tracers have been used, the systematic application of a *suite* of diagnostic geochemical fingerprints—akin to forensic geology—to solve this specific climatic source identification problem is novel. It moves beyond correlation to a mechanistic, provenance-based attribution.

proposals.title: Diagnosing the Source of North Atlantic Freshwater Hosing Events Through Teleconnected Shifts in Southern Hemisphere Westerlies Recorded in Speleothems
proposals.motivation: Freshwater perturbations to the North Atlantic, such as those from glacial meltwater pulses or Arctic runoff, are a primary trigger for abrupt climate change by disrupting the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). However, the paleoclimate record often lacks the specificity to distinguish between different freshwater sources (e.g., Laurentide vs. Greenland melt, or enhanced precipitation), which may have distinct climatic impacts. This ambiguity hinders our ability to fully test and refine coupled climate models used for future projections.
proposals.hypothesis: Different sources and magnitudes of Northern Hemisphere freshwater forcing produce unique, diagnosable shifts in the strength and latitudinal position of the Southern Hemisphere westerly winds, which are sensitively recorded in the geochemistry of mid-latitude Southern Hemisphere speleothems.
proposals.method: We will obtain high-resolution, precisely U/Th-dated speleothem records from key Southern Hemisphere mid-latitude sites (e.g., New Zealand, Patagonia, Tasmania) sensitive to westerly wind-driven precipitation. We will use stable oxygen isotope (δ¹⁸O) gradients between sites and trace element ratios (e.g., Mg/Ca, Sr/Ca) as proxies for wind strength, moisture source, and effective precipitation. These records will be statistically correlated, using cross-wavelet and coherence analysis, with existing, well-dated Northern Hemisphere freshwater event chronologies from marine sediment proxies (e.g., ice-rafted debris layers, neodymium isotopes) and Greenland ice-core records.
proposals.contribution: This work will create a new diagnostic framework for 'fingerprinting' the source of past freshwater forcing events, moving beyond simple detection to source attribution. It will provide critical validation data for climate models by testing their ability to simulate the correct interhemispheric atmospheric teleconnection patterns in response to specific freshwater perturbations. The results will refine our understanding of the sensitivity of the global ocean-atmosphere system, particularly the great ocean conveyor, to different types of forcing.
proposals.novelty: While speleothems are established paleo-rainfall archives, using Southern Hemisphere records as a remote diagnostic tool for discriminating between Northern Hemisphere freshwater sources is a novel inversion of the classic paleoclimate approach. This leverages the well-established but poorly quantified teleconnection that AMOC weakening causes a southward shift of the Intertropical Convergence Zone and a consequent shift in the Southern Hemisphere westerlies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Abrupt Freshwater Forcing in the North Atlantic: An Inverse Mass Balance Approach to Salinity Anomalies
proposals.motivation: Observations indicate a significant freshening of the North Atlantic subpolar gyre, a key region for deep water formation that drives the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC). This freshening, potentially linked to melting ice sheets and increased precipitation, poses a risk of AMOC disruption with major climatic consequences. However, directly measuring the integrated freshwater flux responsible for this large-scale salinity anomaly remains a major observational challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: The observed basin-scale salinity decrease can be used as a conserved tracer anomaly within an inverse mass balance framework to quantitatively diagnose the net freshwater flux required to produce it, providing a novel constraint on the forcing behind potential ocean circulation changes.
proposals.method: We will adapt inverse methods from atmospheric trace gas studies to the ocean's salt and water budget. For a defined North Atlantic control volume, we will construct a forward model of salt conservation incorporating pre-2026 estimates of advective and mixing fluxes from ocean state estimates (e.g., ECCO). Using the observed salinity trend as the target, we will invert this system via a linear least-squares inversion or a Bayesian framework to solve for the unknown net freshwater input (precipitation + runoff + ice melt - evaporation) that closes the budget. The inversion will formally incorporate uncertainties in circulation and the initial salinity observation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, physically consistent estimate of the integrated freshwater forcing impacting the North Atlantic, complementing direct flux measurements. It will directly link the observed oceanic change to its causative forcing, offering a new diagnostic tool for assessing the vulnerability of the AMOC to past and future freshwater perturbations.
proposals.novelty: While box models and forward salinity budgets are common, applying a formal inverse methodology—common in atmospheric chemistry—to solve for freshwater forcing from ocean salinity anomalies is a novel synthesis. This approach treats the ocean basin as a controlled volume for a tracer (salt) inversion, a conceptual shift from typical oceanographic analyses.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Modern Freshwater Forcing of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation: A Test of the Little Ice Age Recovery Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: A leading hypothesis posits that the 20th-century freshening of the subpolar North Atlantic and the inferred slowdown of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) represent a recovery from a stronger, saltier state during the Little Ice Age (LIA). This narrative is foundational to interpreting paleo-proxies and future projections. However, it remains untested against the quantitative freshwater budget from direct anthropogenic warming, including ice sheet melt and hydrological cycle intensification.
proposals.hypothesis: The freshwater flux from 20th-century warming and cryospheric change is sufficient to explain the observed subpolar Atlantic freshening and AMOC weakening, without requiring the system to have started from an anomalously strong LIA state.
proposals.method: We will first construct a detailed freshwater budget for the subpolar North Atlantic from ~1850-2000, synthesizing observed and modeled data on Arctic sea ice export, Greenland runoff, precipitation-evaporation changes, and Pacific-Atlantic throughflow. This net flux will be used to force a calibrated box model of AMOC stability, building on Broecker's conveyor belt concept. The model will be initialized from both a modern baseline and a hypothetical LIA-maximum state. We will then perform an inversion: determine the freshwater forcing required to reproduce the 20th-century freshening trend from each initial state and compare it to our calculated modern budget.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative linkage between the observed 20th-century freshwater forcing and the LIA hypothesis, either validating the recovery narrative or challenging it by showing modern forcing is adequate. It will refine our understanding of AMOC sensitivity to freshwater fluxes and clarify the baseline for projecting future changes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using a forward-calculated, modern freshwater budget as a quantitative test to invert and critically assess a dominant paleoclimatic hypothesis, moving beyond correlation to a mechanistic, forcing-based evaluation.

proposals.title: A Transient Ocean: Diagnosing the Modern Deep-Water Carbonate Ion Distribution as a Legacy of the Last Deglaciation
proposals.motivation: The canonical view of the modern ocean's chemical state is one of steady-state, governed by the long residence times of major ions. However, the ocean's overturning circulation and carbon cycle experienced massive, rapid perturbations during the last deglaciation (the 'Mystery Interval'). If the ocean's chemical adjustment is lagging, the modern observed distribution of carbonate ion (CO3=), a key variable for carbon storage and calcification, may not represent an equilibrium state but a continuing transient.
proposals.hypothesis: The modern deep-water CO3= distribution in the Atlantic and Pacific Oceans is not in steady-state with the current climate, but instead reflects an ongoing chemical relaxation from the deglacial reorganization of the ocean conveyor and terrestrial carbon reservoirs.
proposals.method: We will apply a multi-tracer diagnostic approach using pre-2026 global hydrographic data (GLODAPv2). A 3-D ocean inverse model, constrained by CFC-11/12 transient tracers to quantify modern ventilation ages and pathways, will be used to advect and mix chemical boundary conditions. We will specifically test the compatibility of the observed CO3= field with two scenarios: 1) steady-state with pre-industrial atmospheric CO2, and 2) a transient simulation initialized with a Last Glacial Maximum-like ocean state and forced with a deglacial carbon and circulation history. The mismatch between modeled and observed fields will diagnose the transient signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for separating steady-state from transient chemical signals in the modern ocean, directly testing a core assumption in marine carbon cycle studies. It will recalibrate our interpretation of ocean acidification trends by defining the baseline 'drift' from the last climate transition, and offer a new explanation for deep Pacific carbonate chemistry patterns.
proposals.novelty: While paleo-studies identify past transients, this proposal uniquely posits that a major glacial-interglacial transition imprint is still measurably imprinted on the modern ocean's core chemical field, challenging the steady-state paradigm used in most contemporary models and observations.

proposals.title: Modeling the Little Ice Age Ocean Conveyor Slowdown: A Global Carbon Cycle Test of Deep Atlantic-Pacific Chemical Disequilibrium
proposals.motivation: The 'Great Ocean Conveyor' concept posits that Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) changes can have global climatic impacts. Paleoclimate evidence suggests a significant AMOC weakening during the Little Ice Age (LIA), but the global biogeochemical consequences remain poorly quantified. Testing this hypothesis with models is crucial for understanding how ocean circulation reorganizations, like those potentially triggered by freshwater forcing, affect the global carbon cycle and deep ocean chemistry on centennial timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: That a modeled reduction in North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation, consistent with proposed LIA circulation changes, will produce a measurable, global-scale chemical response in the deep ocean, specifically increasing the carbonate ion concentration ([CO3^2-]) and radiocarbon age of deep Pacific waters relative to the Atlantic.
proposals.method: We will use an established global ocean biogeochemistry model (e.g., the University of Victoria Earth System Climate Model or a modular ocean model coupled to a carbon cycle module). The experimental design will impose a sustained freshwater flux anomaly in the North Atlantic subpolar gyre to suppress NADW formation, mimicking the LIA forcing inferred from paleo-records. We will run multi-century simulations to steady state and analyze the resulting three-dimensional fields of dissolved inorganic carbon, alkalinity, phosphate, oxygen, and the Δ14C of DIC. Key diagnostics will include the inter-basin gradients in deep water [CO3^2-] and the radiocarbon age offset between the deep Atlantic and Pacific basins.
proposals.contribution: This work will quantitatively link a specific paleocirculation change to a predicted global pattern of deep ocean chemistry, providing a mechanistic, model-based framework for interpreting paleo-proxy records (e.g., benthic foraminiferal B/Ca, δ13C, and Δ14C). It will advance our understanding of how the ocean's biological and solubility carbon pumps respond to circulation shifts, informing projections of future carbon cycle feedbacks under potential AMOC changes.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have examined carbon cycle responses to abrupt AMOC collapses (e.g., Heinrich events), this proposal focuses on the more subtle, sustained weakening hypothesized for the LIA, testing its specific signature in the chemical stratification between ocean basins—a direct prediction of the conveyor belt paradigm that has not been fully modeled in a carbon cycle context.

proposals.title: Multi-Tracer Evidence for a Major Reorganization of Deep Pacific Ventilation During the Mystery Interval
proposals.motivation: The 'Mystery Interval' (17.5-14.5 kyr BP) was a period of profound global climate change, yet the role of deep ocean circulation remains poorly constrained. Existing evidence from the Atlantic suggests a significant weakening of the 'great ocean conveyor,' but the response of the deep Pacific—the ocean's largest carbon reservoir—is critical for understanding global carbon cycle feedbacks and remains inadequately documented.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant reduction in North Atlantic Deep Water formation during the Mystery Interval led to a contemporaneous, large-scale change in deep Pacific ventilation and chemistry, which will be recorded as coherent anomalies in the δ13C of benthic foraminifera (reflecting water mass aging and nutrient content) and the Cd/Ca ratio (reflecting phosphate distribution).
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution sediment cores from key deep Pacific sites (e.g., Ontong-Java Plateau, equatorial Pacific) spanning the last deglaciation. We will measure δ13C and Cd/Ca ratios on the same samples of the benthic foraminifera species *Cibicidoides wuellerstorfi* to ensure temporal alignment. The Cd/Ca will be measured via inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) following rigorous cleaning protocols. The multi-proxy records will be compared to existing radiocarbon ventilation proxies and Atlantic records using established age models to test for synchronicity and phasing of circulation changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first multi-tracer test of deep Pacific ventilation changes during the Mystery Interval, directly linking Atlantic forcing to Pacific chemical response. It will quantify the extent of nutrient and carbon reservoir isolation, offering a mechanistic explanation for coeval atmospheric CO2 rise and providing a critical benchmark for climate models simulating ocean circulation under weak Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) states.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have often examined single tracers or focused on the Atlantic, this proposal systematically targets the deep Pacific with a coupled δ13C and Cd/Ca approach specifically for the enigmatic Mystery Interval, seeking a unified chemical signature of a global-scale circulation shift.

proposals.title: The Southern Ocean Conveyor Trigger: Linking North Atlantic Cooling, Westerly Winds, and Enhanced Deep Water Ventilation During the Little Ice Age
proposals.motivation: The Little Ice Age (LIA) represents a significant, recent perturbation to the global climate system, with evidence suggesting a concurrent strengthening of Southern Ocean deep water formation and ventilation. This enhanced ventilation is a critical component of the global ocean 'conveyor' belt, impacting carbon dioxide sequestration and interhemispheric heat exchange. Identifying the specific forcing mechanism for this change is essential for understanding the ocean's role in abrupt climate shifts and for validating coupled climate models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the enhanced Southern Ocean ventilation during the LIA was triggered by a southward shift and intensification of the Southern Hemisphere westerly winds, driven primarily by a sustained cooling and expansion of sea ice in the North Atlantic, which altered the interhemispheric atmospheric pressure gradient.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-proxy, source-to-sink approach. First, we will generate new high-resolution radiocarbon (14C) ventilation ages from carefully selected deep-sea coral (Desmophyllum dianthus) and benthic foraminifera (Cibicidoides spp.) records from the Pacific-Atlantic gateway and the Antarctic Circumpolar Current pathway. Second, we will analyze the neodymium isotope (εNd) composition of the same samples to trace water mass provenance and mixing. Third, we will integrate these new oceanic data with existing terrestrial paleoclimate archives (e.g., lacustrine records from the Great Basin and Patagonia) and reanalyze ice core aerosol fluxes to reconstruct Southern Hemisphere wind strength and latitudinal position. The correlation and phasing of these records will be tested using established statistical methods for time-series analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first directly dated, multi-proxy reconstruction of Southern Ocean ventilation dynamics during the LIA, explicitly testing the linkage between Northern Hemisphere forcing and Southern Ocean response. It will deliver a mechanistic framework for how the 'great ocean conveyor' is modulated during centennial-scale cooling events, offering a critical benchmark for Earth System Models.
proposals.novelty: While past studies have noted LIA changes in the Southern Ocean, this proposal uniquely seeks to determine the *trigger* by combining novel deep-sea coral 14C-εNd pairs with terrestrial wind proxies to establish causality and phasing, moving beyond correlation to a process-based explanation.

proposals.title: Quantifying Signal Attenuation in the Deep-Sea Archive: A Sediment Transport Modeling Approach to Assess Bioturbation and Geochemical Fidelity in North Atlantic and Pacific Cores
proposals.motivation: The fidelity of geochemical signals in deep-sea sediment cores, crucial for reconstructing past ocean circulation and climate events like those during the Mystery Interval, is compromised by post-depositional processes. Bioturbation and physical mixing can significantly attenuate or distort the original climate signal, leading to potential misinterpretations of the rate and magnitude of past changes, such as those recorded in the 'great ocean conveyor'.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying quantitative sediment transport models to deep-sea core data will reveal systematic, depth-dependent biases in signal preservation, and that the degree of signal attenuation differs significantly between the more dynamic North Atlantic and the more quiescent Pacific deep-sea depositional environments.
proposals.method: We will apply a one-dimensional, time-dependent advection-diffusion-reaction model, specifically the 'Bioturbation-Advection Model' (BAM), to high-resolution geochemical (e.g., δ¹⁸O, trace metals) and physical property data from selected North Atlantic and Pacific cores. The model will be parameterized using core-specific sedimentation rates and will explicitly simulate bioturbation as a diffusive process, with mixing coefficients (Db) constrained by excess ²¹⁰Pb and ²³⁴Th profiles where available. We will run inverse model simulations to recover the original input signal and quantify the attenuation of key climate events.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework for assessing the reliability of paleoceanographic proxies, directly informing the interpretation of events like abrupt climate changes and extinctions recorded in marine sediments. It will yield site-specific 'filter functions' that can be applied to deconvolve mixed signals, thereby improving the accuracy of climate reconstructions derived from the deep-sea archive.
proposals.novelty: While mixing models are standard in sedimentology, their rigorous application to deconvolve geochemical climate signals in deep-sea cores, and the systematic comparison of signal fidelity between major ocean basins in the context of the global conveyor, represents a novel synthesis of geological and paleoceanographic methodologies.

proposals.title: Decadal Variability in Southern Ocean Ventilation as a Potential Precursor to Abrupt Climate Shifts: A Modern Analog for the Little Ice Age Trigger
proposals.motivation: The 'great ocean conveyor' is a key component of global climate, and its past disruptions, such as a proposed slowdown in North Atlantic Deep Water (NADW) formation, are hypothesized to have triggered events like the Little Ice Age. The Southern Ocean is the primary region where deep waters upwell and are re-ventilated, yet its role in modulating the conveyor's stability on societally relevant timescales is poorly constrained. Understanding modern interannual-to-decadal variability in Southern Ocean ventilation is critical for assessing whether it can provide early warning signals for larger, centennial-scale climate shifts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modern decadal-scale anomalies in Southern Ocean ventilation, particularly in the upwelling and subduction zones of the Pacific and Atlantic sectors, exhibit patterns that prefigure the mechanism proposed for a conveyor slowdown during the Little Ice Age. Specifically, we posit that shifts in wind-driven upwelling and water mass mixing can alter the density structure feeding into the Atlantic, thereby modulating NADW formation precursors.
proposals.method: We will analyze the historical record (1980-2025) using high-resolution ocean reanalysis products (e.g., ECCO, ORAS) and observational datasets (Argo, satellite altimetry). The core method involves calculating ventilation rates and water mass transformation rates in key Southern Ocean sectors using Lagrangian particle tracking and Eulerian diagnostic frameworks. We will specifically apply mixing analysis to quantify changes in the properties of Subantarctic Mode Water and Antarctic Intermediate Water, tracking their northward export into the Atlantic and Pacific basins. Statistical correlation and lead-lag analysis will then link Southern Ocean variability indices to changes in deep water formation signals in the North Atlantic.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly connect modern observational oceanography to paleoclimate hypotheses, providing a mechanistic test of the Southern Ocean's role as a potential pacemaker for abrupt change. It will produce the first quantitative assessment of whether decadal ventilation shifts are of sufficient magnitude to prefigure major conveyor belt adjustments. The findings will refine climate models by identifying key observational targets for monitoring ocean-climate stability.
proposals.novelty: While Broecker's conveyor concept focuses on the North Atlantic as a trigger, this study uniquely inverts the perspective to investigate the Southern Ocean as a potential source of preparatory variability. It applies modern, high-resolution data and water mass tracking methods—typically used for contemporary circulation studies—to explicitly test a paleoclimate mechanism, bridging a critical gap between these disciplines.

proposals.title: Inverting the Glacial Ocean Conveyor: A Formal Inverse Model to Constrain Atlantic-Pacific Deep Water Exchange from Carbonate Ion Distributions
proposals.motivation: Reconstructing past ocean circulation is critical for understanding climate variability, but direct proxies for flow are scarce. The distribution of carbonate ion (CO3=) in deep-sea sediments, a well-established chemical proxy for deep water mass age and corrosivity, provides an integrated record of circulation and carbon cycle processes. Formally inverting this chemical field to solve for circulation represents a powerful, untapped approach to test hypotheses about the structure of the glacial 'conveyor' and the origins of deep water during events like the Mystery Interval.
proposals.hypothesis: The spatial pattern of glacial deep ocean CO3=, primarily derived from sediment-based proxies like benthic foraminiferal B/Ca and dissolution indices, can be inverted to yield a quantitative circulation solution that reveals a dominant northern source for deep Pacific water and significantly altered Atlantic-Pacific exchange relative to the modern.
proposals.method: We will adapt an ocean inverse modeling framework, building on established box-model and geostrophic inverse techniques used in modern oceanography. The core algorithm will treat the steady-state glacial ocean as a network of boxes connected by advective and diffusive fluxes. The forward model will simulate CO3= distributions based on pre-2026 understanding of alkalinity, nutrients, and respiration. A linear inverse method, minimizing the misfit between modeled and proxy-derived CO3= under mass-balance constraints, will solve for the unknown circulation fluxes and source water signatures. Sensitivity tests will target key unknowns: the strength of North Pacific deep water formation and the throughflow of southern-sourced water into the North Atlantic.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative circulation field explicitly derived from paleo-chemical data, moving beyond qualitative tracer comparisons. It will provide a rigorous test for the 'nutrient deepening' and 'separated conveyors' hypotheses for the glacial carbon cycle. The method establishes a formal framework for integrating disparate geochemical proxies to solve for past ocean dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While chemical tracers have long been used to *infer* circulation, a formal mathematical inversion of a paleo-chemical field to directly *solve for* circulation fluxes is a novel synthesis. This approach treats the ocean's chemical distribution as the known output of an integrated system, with circulation as the unknown variable to be optimized.

ideas.title: Study dual role of Southern Ocean vs Pacific
ideas.description: Compare the relative timing and strength of Southern Ocean and North Pacific ventilation changes during deglaciation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Challenge the North Atlantic-Southern Ocean paradigm
ideas.description: Test if the North Pacific role necessitates a re-evaluation of traditional deglacial carbon cycle drivers.

ideas.title: Determine the trigger for enhanced Southern Ocean ventilation
ideas.description: Identify the climatic or oceanic forcing that caused stronger deep water formation during the LIA.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was a change in thermohaline circulation responsible for the Little Ice Age?

ideas.title: Volcanically Synchronized Paleo-Reconstruction of the Atlantic-Southern Ocean Ventilation Seesaw
ideas.description: This project uses globally synchronous volcanic sulfate markers from precisely dated polar ice cores as absolute chronological tie-points to synchronize high-resolution marine sediment records from the North Atlantic and Southern Ocean. This precise synchronization enables the direct, quantitative comparison of ventilation proxies (e.g., δ13C, 14C, Nd isotopes) from both basins on a common timescale. The approach will empirically determine the phasing, magnitude, and lead-lag relationships of deep water production changes during key climatic transitions, providing a robust test for the existence and dynamics of the interhemispheric ventilation seesaw.

ideas.title: Disentangling Climatic and Intrinsic Oceanic Drivers of Abyssal Ventilation Shifts During the Little Ice Age
ideas.description: This research will use high-resolution geochemical proxy records from the Southern Ocean to reconstruct not just the mean ventilation rate during the Little Ice Age (LIA), but also its variability and abrupt shifts. By analyzing these reconstructions in conjunction with climate model simulations that isolate external forcing from internal variability, the project will specifically test if rapid deep salinity and ventilation changes were a direct, linear response to LIA cooling or emerged from internal ocean dynamics, such as shifts in eddy activity or deep western boundary current stability. This approach reframes the LIA as a natural experiment to quantify the relative roles of external forcing versus intrinsic oceanic processes in driving abrupt deep ocean change.

ideas.title: Scale up to global ocean carbon redistribution
ideas.description: Model how a North Pacific event affects the whole ocean's carbon inventory and ventilation age globally.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Test the hypothesis with global carbon cycle models
ideas.description: Incorporate the proposed LIA circulation change into models to see if it reproduces deep ocean chemistry.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was a change in thermohaline circulation responsible for the Little Ice Age?

ideas.title: Downscale to study convection's biogeochemical effects
ideas.description: Investigate the local ecological and biogeochemical consequences of deep mixing in the North Pacific.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Extinction timing was diachronous across depths
ideas.description: Challenge the synchronous decline assumption by analyzing a depth transect of sites within the South Atlantic.
ideas.sourceTitle: The great ocean conveyor

ideas.title: Challenge constant glacial-interglacial salinity gradients
ideas.description: Investigate if assumed stable seawater oxygen isotope-salinity relationships held in the tropical glacial ocean.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cooling the tropics

ideas.title: Reconcile Atlantic-Southern Ocean Seesaw Dynamics Using Inverted Tracer Sensitivities
ideas.description: This research direction combines the need to recalibrate paleo-circulation proxies (CFC-11, 14C, PO4*) with the investigation of the Atlantic-Southern Ocean deep water production seesaw. By first inverting the cause of modern tracer discordance—using observed CFC-11 ventilation rates to redefine the sensitivities of radiocarbon and PO4* in models—we establish a revised, physically-consistent tracer framework. This updated framework is then applied to paleo-records and model simulations of the last deglaciation to search for and quantify anti-phased deep water production signals. The synergy allows us to move from qualitative pattern matching to a quantitative, tracer-constrained assessment of whether the seesaw is a dominant mode of overturning circulation variability, resolving a key uncertainty in past climate dynamics.

ideas.title: Fjord-Scale Laboratory for the Atlantic-Southern Ocean Seesaw
ideas.description: This research direction uses fjord and marginal sea systems as dynamic, real-world laboratories to investigate the fundamental processes driving anti-phased deep water formation. By instrumenting modern fjords that exhibit rapid switching between estuarine and anti-estuarine circulation (analogous to Atlantic downwelling and Southern Ocean upwelling modes), researchers can directly measure the precise atmospheric and hydrological forcings that trigger shifts in deep water production. The high-resolution sedimentary records within these fjords then provide a tightly coupled paleo-archive to test whether these observed modern process switches correlate with past anti-phased behavior between fjord basins, validating the seesaw mechanism's core dynamics at a manageable scale. This approach enables targeted process studies that can directly inform and constrain global climate models of the Atlantic-Southern Ocean seesaw.

ideas.title: Applying Paleoclimate Signal Deconvolution to Challenge Constant Ventilation Assumptions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption of constant Southern Ocean ventilation over the past 800 years in interpretations of radiocarbon and phosphate-starved water mass (P04*) tracer records. By drawing an analogy from paleoclimatology—specifically the solution to the analogous problem of challenging constant gas-age-ice-age differences (Δage) in ice cores—we adapt a multi-proxy deconvolution method. The analogous ice core solution used independent climate proxies (methane, δ¹⁵N) to inversely model a variable Δage. Adapted here, we propose using a suite of independent paleoclimate records (e.g., from sediment cores and ice cores: sea ice proxies, dust fluxes, temperature reconstructions) that reflect potential drivers of ventilation (wind stress, buoyancy forcing, sea ice dynamics). These proxies will serve as simultaneous constraints in an inverse model to deconvolve the radiocarbon/P04* signals, testing the hypothesis that ventilation varied in response to climatic forcing like Southern Annular Mode shifts or buoyancy changes, rather than remaining constant. This approach transforms the tracers from passive recorders interpreted under a static assumption into dynamic variables within a coupled physical-biogeochemical reconstruction.

ideas.title: Quantify relative roles of physical vs biological carbon uptake
ideas.description: Directly measure and compare the ocean's physical and biological processes for absorbing anthropogenic CO2.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was the Medieval Warm Period Global?

ideas.title: Apply a 'Tree-Ring Crossdating' Approach to Southern Ocean Sediment Cores
ideas.description: This idea adapts the crossdating solution from dendrochronology to test the 20th-century slowdown hypothesis in Southern Ocean deep water formation. The analogous problem in tree-ring science—building precise, long chronologies from multiple overlapping but imperfect records—is solved by pattern-matching annual growth variations. Here, we propose using ultra-high-resolution sediment cores (e.g., from varved sediments or those scanned with XRF at sub-millimeter resolution) to identify annual-layer proxy patterns (like diatom blooms or lithogenic particle fluxes influenced by surface conditions tied to deep convection). By applying crossdating techniques to match these patterns across a network of cores from different regions (e.g., the Weddell Sea, Ross Sea, and Antarctic Peninsula), we can construct a synchronized, absolutely dated multi-proxy network for the last 300-500 years. This approach minimizes the dating errors that plague conventional radiometric methods, enabling a direct and statistically robust test of whether deep water formation rates exhibited a unique slowdown or increased variability during the 20th century compared to the preceding centuries.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Global Carbon Cycle Impact of Multi-Decadal Oscillations in Southern Ocean Ventilation
ideas.description: This research integrates the question of trend-versus-oscillation into the global biogeochemical modeling framework. It will use Earth system models to simulate the global carbon cycle response under two distinct scenarios: a sustained, monotonic 20th-century slowdown in Southern Ocean ventilation versus a slowdown that is part of a known multi-decadal oscillation (e.g., linked to modes like the Southern Annular Mode or Interdecadal Pacific Oscillation). By comparing the magnitude, spatial pattern, and persistence of resulting atmospheric CO2 anomalies and ocean carbon storage changes between these scenarios, the work will directly quantify the climate risk and carbon budget uncertainty attributable to the oscillatory nature of Southern Ocean dynamics.

ideas.title: Quantify NPDW impact on global carbon cycle
ideas.description: Use Earth system models to detail how North Pacific deep water formation affects global ocean carbon storage and isotopes.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Link ventilation changes to ice-rafting cycles
ideas.description: Examine if Southern Ocean slowdowns correlate with specific phases of the 1500-year ice-rafting cycle.
ideas.sourceTitle: A possible 20th-century slowdown of southern ocean deep water formation

ideas.title: A Multi-Archive Network and Data Assimilation Framework to Quantify 20th-Century Southern Ocean Overturning Change
ideas.description: This project constructs a spatially explicit reconstruction of Southern Ocean deep water formation and export variability over the past 500-1000 years. It integrates a strategically designed network of high-resolution sediment cores (for direct bottom current and water mass proxies) with synchronized ice core records of marine aerosol fluxes and tree-ring constrained atmospheric CO2 growth rates, which provide integrative atmospheric constraints on ocean ventilation. These heterogeneous proxy data are fused within an ensemble data assimilation framework, using a lightweight ocean model, to produce a probabilistic reconstruction that distinguishes basin-scale overturning changes from local hydrographic shifts and rigorously quantifies reconstruction uncertainty.

ideas.title: Investigating the Atlantic's Compensatory Response to Southern Forcing via a Metabolic Feedback Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea via analogical reasoning from systems physiology. The analogous problem is how one subsystem (e.g., cardiovascular) compensates for a dysfunction in another (e.g., respiratory) to maintain systemic homeostasis. The analogous solution involves identifying and quantifying the specific feedback parameters (rate, sensitivity, lag time) of the compensatory mechanism. Adapted to oceanography, the study will investigate if and how North Atlantic deep water formation actively compensated for a Southern Ocean slowdown. Using paleoclimate proxy data and coupled climate model experiments, the research will test the hypothesis that changes in NADW acted not merely as a concurrent change, but as a compensatory feedback, modulating the global impact of the Southern slowdown on ocean heat redistribution, carbon sequestration, and climate stability. The core question becomes: Did the Atlantic response exhibit the hallmarks of a homeostatic mechanism?

ideas.title: Quantifying the Spectrum of Phase Relationships in Atlantic and Southern Ocean Deep Water Production Across Climate Transitions
ideas.description: This project moves beyond testing a simple anti-phase seesaw to systematically characterize the complete range of phase relationships (synchronous, anti-phase, lagged, uncorrelated) between North Atlantic Deep Water and Antarctic Bottom Water production. We will achieve this by integrating a perturbed-parameter ensemble of climate model simulations with a newly compiled network of high-resolution, radiometrically constrained paleo-proxy records (e.g., Nd isotopes, δ13C, sortable silt) from both ocean basins. A Bayesian statistical framework will then be used to identify the dominant spatial and temporal modes of deep water production co-variability during key Pleistocene climate transitions, determining under what conditions a seesaw mechanism emerges versus other dynamical relationships.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Attribution of Deep Freshening to Source-Specific Anomalies via Lagrangian Pathways
ideas.description: This research employs a coupled observational and high-resolution modeling approach to move beyond correlation and establish causation in deep-ocean freshening patterns. A Lagrangian particle-tracking system, initialized within a state-of-the-art ocean model, is used to trace the pathways of freshwater anomalies from specific surface source regions (e.g., Greenland meltwater, Arctic export, precipitation anomalies) into the interior deep water masses. By quantifying the relative contribution of each source pathway to the freshening signal in distinct water masses like North Atlantic Deep Water and Antarctic Bottom Water, the study directly tests the mechanistic hypothesis that advective pathways structure the non-uniform response. This framework enables the attribution of observed deep salinity changes to specific surface forcing mechanisms and their evolving oceanic routes.

ideas.title: Challenge the steady-state assumption for modern ocean
ideas.description: Investigate if the modern deep ocean CO3= distribution itself reflects a recent, ongoing transient.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was a change in thermohaline circulation responsible for the Little Ice Age?

ideas.title: Ocean Freshening as a Primary Climate Driver: A Neuroendocrine Analogy
ideas.description: This idea inverts the cause-effect assumption that ocean freshening is merely a response to climate change by drawing an analogy from neuroendocrinology. In that field, the paradigm was shifted by discovering that the hypothalamus, not the pituitary, was the primary driver via specific releasing hormones. Analogously, we propose that deep ocean freshening from sources like polar ice melt could act as a similar primary 'releasing signal' for the climate system. The adapted research idea is to identify the specific freshwater flux thresholds and injection locations that non-linearly trigger major shifts in ocean circulation (e.g., AMOC collapse) and subsequent global climate reorganization. This frames freshening as an active, initiating driver capable of setting the future climate state, fundamentally challenging its standard treatment as a slow feedback.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Attribution of Climate Forcing by Anthropogenic Ocean Freshening
ideas.description: This research program seeks to rigorously test whether large-scale ocean freshening can act as a primary climate forcing mechanism, not just a feedback. It employs a hierarchy of climate models to isolate and quantify specific physical pathways, such as freshwater-driven shutdown of deep-water formation and subsequent planetary heat redistribution. The approach formally applies detection and attribution methodologies to both paleoclimate intervals (e.g., the 8.2ka event) and 21st-century projections to statistically distinguish the climatic impact of freshening from other drivers. The goal is to produce a mechanistic, quantified assessment of freshening as an independent climate forcing agent within the Earth system.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Deduce past circulation from chemistry alone
ideas.description: Formally invert the deep ocean CO3= distribution to solve for the unknown past circulation and source waters.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was a change in thermohaline circulation responsible for the Little Ice Age?

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Deduce pump strength from carbon data
ideas.description: Infer the historical strength of the biological pump from atmospheric and oceanic carbon records.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was the Medieval Warm Period Global?

ideas.title: Refine boron isotope data and interpretations
ideas.description: Improve the boron isotope dataset to better constrain the pH and carbon chemistry changes during the proposed convection event.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Inferring Freshwater Forcing from Salinity Anomalies Using Mass Balance Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse modeling approach common in atmospheric chemistry to quantify the freshwater flux responsible for an observed ocean salinity decrease. In atmospheric studies, the concentration anomaly of a well-mixed tracer is used in conjunction with transport models to inversely calculate the strength of an unknown source or sink. Here, the ocean basin is the control volume, the salinity is the conserved tracer, and the observed salinity decrease is the anomaly. By constructing an inverse model of the basin's salt and water mass balance—which includes terms for ocean circulation, mixing, and known surface exchanges—the system can be solved backwards to infer the net freshwater input (the unknown 'source') needed to explain the observed freshening. This method provides a quantitative estimate of the freshwater forcing that is consistent with both the observed change and the underlying physics of the system.

ideas.title: Use sediment proxy data assimilation with climate models
ideas.description: Incorporate the extinction and sediment records as constraints in data assimilation frameworks for mid-Pleistocene climate models.
ideas.sourceTitle: The great ocean conveyor

ideas.title: Use neodymium isotopes to trace water masses
ideas.description: Apply neodymium isotope analyses on the same foraminifera to directly trace the provenance of deep waters.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lacustrine cave carbonates: Novel archives of paleohydrologic change in the Bonneville Basin (Utah, USA)

ideas.title: Fingerprinting Freshwater Sources Using Multi-Tracer Isotopic and Geochemical Signatures
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the forensic fingerprinting methodology from geology to solve the climatic problem of identifying the source of oceanic freshening. Just as forensic geologists use a suite of diagnostic isotopic and geochemical tracers to pinpoint the provenance of a soil sample, this project proposes to develop distinct multi-parameter signatures for Arctic runoff, Greenland ice melt, and precipitation-derived freshwater. The adapted solution involves systematically measuring and compiling key tracers—including water stable isotopes, dissolved ion and nutrient ratios, and lithogenic isotopes—that are characteristic of each source's unique formation pathway and geological context. By applying this fingerprinting library to seawater samples from the freshening region, the research aims to deconvolve and quantify the relative contributions from the three candidate sources, thereby identifying the specific climatic perturbation driver.

ideas.title: Quantify the bias and uncertainty in ocean climate trends from seasonal sampling gaps
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a conceptual critique to empirically test and quantify the impact of seasonal sampling. It uses existing multi-year, full-depth, year-round physical and biogeochemical data from instrumented moorings and profiling floats to construct a complete climatological and trend baseline. By systematically subsampling this data to mimic summer-only (or other seasonal) occupation patterns, it directly calculates the bias and increased uncertainty in derived long-term trends for properties like temperature, salinity, and oxygen. The output is a framework that evaluates under what conditions (e.g., region, depth, variable) seasonal sampling is insufficient and provides correction factors or uncertainty penalties for trend analyses reliant on such data.

ideas.title: Challenge constant ventilation over 800 years
ideas.description: Assume radiocarbon/P04* reflect variable, not constant, Southern Ocean ventilation over the past 800 years.
ideas.sourceTitle: A possible 20th-century slowdown of southern ocean deep water formation

ideas.title: Internal ocean dynamics as the reconciling mechanism for multi-tracer ventilation timescales
ideas.description: This research posits that the apparent discordance between modern ventilation tracers like CFC-11 and centennial-scale tracers like radiocarbon or P04* in the deep ocean can be resolved by internal ocean dynamics. Specifically, we hypothesize that rapid, localized events driven by intrinsic ocean variability—such as shifts in deep western boundary currents, eddy-driven overturning anomalies, or abyssal water mass injections—can cause swift changes in salinity and other deep properties, imprinting strongly on short-lived tracers while leaving a different, smoothed signature on integrated tracers. By developing an integrated model that explicitly simulates these internal dynamic regimes and their transient tracer fields, the work aims to demonstrate that rapid deep ocean change is fundamentally a dynamic phenomenon, reconciling tracer timescales and challenging a purely climatically forced interpretation of ventilation.

ideas.title: Question the representativeness of foraminifera records
ideas.description: Test if benthic foraminifera used for deep temperatures lived in microhabitats with atypical conditions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cooling the tropics

ideas.title: Scale up to global carbon cycle impact
ideas.description: Model the effect of removing this benthic foraminiferal functional group on carbon burial and remineralization across the MPT.
ideas.sourceTitle: The great ocean conveyor

ideas.title: Quantify the Modern Warming Freshwater Forcing Required to Override a Putative Little Ice Age AMOC State
ideas.description: This research direction combines quantitative freshwater budgeting with a critical examination of the Little Ice Age hypothesis. It calculates the freshwater flux required to produce the observed 20th-century subpolar Atlantic freshening and associated AMOC slowdown. Crucially, it uses this calculated modern forcing to test whether it is sufficient to drive the system from a hypothetical Little Ice Age maximum state, or if the observed changes are instead primarily a direct response to contemporary warming and ice melt, effectively bypassing the need for a LIA recovery narrative. The synergy lies in using a forward calculation (Idea A) to perform a novel inversion test on a dominant historical hypothesis (Idea B).

ideas.title: Mechanistic Test of Ice-Rafting Forcing on Southern Ocean Ventilation Using Synchronous Proxies and Model Experiments
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlation to test the mechanistic hypothesis that freshwater pulses from Northern Hemisphere ice-rafting events directly force Southern Ocean ventilation slowdowns. We generate synchronous, high-resolution proxy records of ventilation (Δ14C, epibenthic δ13C) and ice-rafted debris from strategically located Southern Ocean sediment cores to establish precise phasing. These empirical constraints are then used to design and evaluate freshwater perturbation experiments in an intermediate-complexity climate model with an ocean tracer, isolating the physical causality of the proposed teleconnection and controlling for common external drivers.

ideas.title: Scale down: Study decadal modern Southern Ocean variability
ideas.description: Analyze if modern interannual-decadal shifts in Southern Ocean ventilation prefigure the proposed LIA mechanism.
ideas.sourceTitle: Was a change in thermohaline circulation responsible for the Little Ice Age?

ideas.title: Fingerprint Northern Hemisphere freshwater forcing via Southern Hemisphere speleothem wind reconstructions
ideas.description: This research direction uses high-resolution speleothem geochemical records from the Southern Hemisphere mid-latitudes to reconstruct past changes in wind strength and position. By precisely dating periods of intensified or shifted Southern Hemisphere westerlies—a known teleconnected response to North Atlantic freshwater forcing—the project aims to indirectly identify the specific source (Arctic runoff, Greenland melt, or precipitation) of the freshwater perturbation. The synergy lies in correlating the timing and pattern of Southern Hemisphere wind anomalies from speleothems with independent evidence of Northern Hemisphere freshwater events, thereby creating a diagnostic tool to discriminate between freshening sources based on their unique remote atmospheric impacts.

ideas.title: Provenance-constrained interglacial ventilation dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction applies the high-resolution sediment provenance tools developed for ice-rafted debris (IRD) to systematically investigate ocean ventilation slowdowns during previous interglacials, such as MIS 5e and MIS 11. By fingerprinting the geological source of IRD layers, the work will determine if ventilation weakening events were consistently triggered by discharge from specific, vulnerable ice sheet sectors. This approach tests the hypothesis that a recurring sequence of ice sheet instability in a particular region is a fundamental pacing mechanism for interglacial ocean circulation, providing a process-based framework for understanding climate dynamics across warm periods.

ideas.title: Apply coral Sr/Ca thermometry to glacial periods

ideas.sourceTitle: Cooling the tropics

ideas.title: Apply Dendrochronological Crossdating to Speleothem Climate Series
ideas.description: This idea adapts the crossdating methodology from dendrochronology to the domain of speleothem paleoclimatology. In tree-ring science, crossdating is the fundamental technique for precisely aligning multiple records to create a robust, continuous, and absolutely dated master chronology by matching patterns of ring-width or isotopic variability. The analogous problem in speleothem research is aligning and validating high-resolution geochemical series (e.g., δ¹⁸O, Mg/Ca, Sr/Ca) from multiple stalagmites to reconstruct a coherent climate signal, such as Southern Hemisphere winds driving ocean ventilation. The adapted solution involves applying statistical cross-correlation and pattern-matching algorithms to these speleothem geochemical time-series from the same cave or climatic region. This 'speleothem crossdating' will synchronize individual records, identify and correct for non-climatic disturbances or growth pauses, and ultimately construct a master, validated chronology. The resulting composite record will provide a more reliable and high-fidelity reconstruction of past wind variability and its role in ventilating the Southern Ocean, transferring a powerful methodological framework from one high-resolution climate archive to another.

ideas.title: Applying Phylogenetic Comparative Methods to Interglacial Ventilation Signatures
ideas.description: This idea adapts phylogenetic comparative methods from evolutionary biology to the problem of scaling ventilation slowdown signatures to previous interglacials. In biology, these methods are used to distinguish convergent evolution from shared ancestry by mapping traits onto a phylogenetic tree. Here, we construct a conceptual 'climate state tree' representing the sequence and forcing conditions of past interglacials (e.g., MIS 5e, 11, 19). We then statistically map ventilation slowdown evidence from paleo-proxies (e.g., δ13C, Pa/Th) onto this framework. Using adapted comparative models, we can test whether slowdowns are statistically linked to specific forcing thresholds or configurations common across interglacials, thereby identifying the necessary conditions for Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation (AMOC) weakening and providing a more robust, phylogenetically-informed analog for future projections.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Drivers of Atlantic-Southern Ocean Ventilation Coupling: A Water Mass Transformation Framework
ideas.description: This research moves beyond a conceptual relaxation of the seesaw to a mechanistic investigation of the conditions enabling coupled or decoupled deep ventilation. Using a hierarchy of ocean and climate models, it applies a water mass transformation analysis to quantify the separate contributions of surface buoyancy fluxes, interior mixing, and isopycnal processes to ventilation in the Atlantic and Southern Ocean. The approach systematically tests hypotheses—like the role of Pacific stratification or Southern Hemisphere westerly winds—that could break the canonical anti-phase link, providing a physics-based, testable foundation for understanding past and future ventilation regimes.

ideas.title: Apply Electrical Circuit Theory to Model the Atlantic-Southern Ocean Seesaw
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from electrical engineering to investigate the Atlantic-Southern Ocean deep water production seesaw. The analogous problem—managing anti-phased current flow in parallel circuit branches—is solved using circuit theory principles like Kirchhoff's law and impedance. We adapt this by modeling the global overturning circulation as a closed circuit where the total deep water formation 'current' is driven by climate forcing. The North Atlantic and Southern Ocean are represented as two branches with variable 'climatic impedance.' The model will mathematically describe how increased impedance (e.g., from freshwater freshening) in one branch reduces its deep water production while simultaneously increasing it in the other, creating the observed anti-phased seesaw. This theoretical framework can then be tested and parameterized using paleo-oceanographic proxy data or coupled climate model outputs.

ideas.title: Causal Pathways: Testing for Compensatory vs. Coupled AMOC Response to Southern Ocean Perturbations
ideas.description: This study moves beyond correlation to establish causality in inter-basin overturning interactions. Using a targeted model hierarchy, we impose controlled Southern Ocean wind and buoyancy flux perturbations to simulate a slowdown. We then employ Lagrangian diagnostics and novel tracer releases to rigorously trace the fate of North Atlantic water masses, quantifying any change in NADW formation rate, density, and southward export. The core objective is to determine if the North Atlantic response is compensatory (increased overturning), neutral, or coupled (further decreased overturning), thereby identifying the dominant dynamical pathway and its implications for global overturning stability.

ideas.title: Search for NPDW events in other deglaciations
